entry.content:
з ”з©¶иғҢжҷҜ

йҮ‘еұһй”ӮдҪңиҙҹжһҒзҡ„жҢ‘жҲҳ

  • й”Ӯжһқжҷ¶з”ҹй•ҝ
    • еӣәжҖҒз”өи§ЈиҙЁеҸҜжҠ‘еҲ¶й”Ӯжһқжҷ¶з”ҹй•ҝ
    • иҝҮе……orеҝ«е……ж—¶гҖҒд»ҘйҮ‘еұһй”ӮдҪңиҙҹжһҒж—¶
  • дҪ“з§ҜиҶЁиғҖдёҺз”өжһҒзІүеҢ–

еӣәжҖҒз”өжұ вҖ”вҖ”йҮҮз”ЁеӣәжҖҒз”өи§ЈиҙЁзҡ„й”ӮзҰ»еӯҗз”өжұ 

  • з”өеҢ–еӯҰзӘ—еҸЈпјҢ5V>4.2V

  • йҮ‘еұһLiдҪңиҙҹжһҒеҸҜиғҪе®һзҺ°

  • й«ҳй”ӮзҰ»еӯҗз”өеҜјзҺҮ

  • дҪҺз”өеӯҗз”өеҜјзҺҮ

  • жӯЈиҙҹжһҒдјҳејӮзҡ„еҢ–еӯҰе’Ңз”өеҢ–еӯҰзЁіе®ҡжҖ§

  • йҡҫиЎЁеҫҒ

  • жҹ”жҖ§дёҚеӨҹ

  • дёҚеҘҪеӣһж”¶

  • еӣәжҖҒз”өи§ЈиҙЁзҡ„з”өеҢ–еӯҰеҲҶи§ЈпјҢдёҚеҸҜйҒҝе…Қ

зЎ«еҢ–зү©еӣәжҖҒз”өи§ЈиҙЁ

дјҳеҠҝ

  • жӣҙй«ҳзҡ„зҰ»еӯҗз”өеҜјзҺҮ
  • иүҜеҘҪзҡ„з•ҢйқўжҺҘи§Ұ

дёҚи¶і

  • з”өеҺӢзӘ—еҸЈиҫғзӘ„
    • еӣәжҖҒз”өи§ЈиҙЁеңЁдёҚеҸ‘з”ҹз”өеҢ–еӯҰеҲҶи§Јзҡ„жғ…еҶөдёӢиғҪеӨҹзЁіе®ҡе·ҘдҪңзҡ„з”өеҺӢиҢғеӣҙ
  • еҢ–еӯҰеӨұж•ҲвҖ”вҖ”з”өжһҒ/з”өи§ЈиҙЁз•Ңйқўе…је®№жҖ§е·®
  • й”Ӯжһқжҷ¶еӨұж•Ҳ
  • жңәжў°еӨұж•Ҳ
  • вҖңеңЁжӯЈжһҒеј•е…ҘдҝқжҠӨж¶ӮеұӮжҳҜе®һзҺ°ASSLBsе®һйҷ…еә”з”Ёзҡ„жңҖжңүж•Ҳж–№жі•вҖқ
  • ж— жі•еӨ§и§„жЁЎеҲ¶йҖ пјҹи¶…и–„з”өи§ЈиҙЁиҶңеҲ¶еӨҮеӣ°йҡҫпјҢж— жңәз”өи§ЈиҙЁзҡ„еҠӣеӯҰжҖ§иғҪе·®

ж”№жҖ§

  • з”өи§ЈиҙЁиЎЁйқўж¶ӮеұӮжқҘжҠ‘еҲ¶з•ҢйқўеҸҚеә”пјҲеҰӮGOпјү
  • з»“жһ„и®ҫи®ЎпјҡйҖҡиҝҮеўһеҠ з”өи§ЈиҙЁзҡ„еҜҶеәҰе’ҢдјҳеҢ–еҫ®и§Ӯз»“жһ„жқҘжҠ‘еҲ¶й”Ӯжһқжҷ¶з”ҹй•ҝгҖӮдҫӢеҰӮпјҢйҖҡиҝҮдёӨжӯҘзғ§з»“жі•еҲ¶еӨҮзҡ„Li6PS5Clз”өи§ЈиҙЁе…·жңү6.11 mS cm-1зҡ„й«ҳзҰ»еӯҗдј еҜјзҺҮе’ҢиүҜеҘҪзҡ„еҫӘзҺҜзЁіе®ҡжҖ§
  • жҺәжқӮжқҘжҸҗй«ҳз”өи§ЈиҙЁзҡ„зЁіе®ҡжҖ§е’ҢзҰ»еӯҗдј еҜјзҺҮ
  • ж¶үеҸҠй”ӮеҗҲйҮ‘иҙҹжһҒжқҘеҮҸе°‘дҪ“з§ҜиҶЁиғҖпјҢеўһеҠ з•Ңйқўзӣёе®№жҖ§
  • дҪҺжҲҗжң¬зӯ–з•Ҙпјҹ
    • LiCl + Na2S вҶ’ Li2S + NaCl
ж–№жі•дјҳзӮ№зјәзӮ№
жңәжў°зҗғзЈЁжі•е·Ҙиүәз®ҖеҚ•пјҢе®һйӘҢе®Өжҳ“ж“ҚдҪңжҳ“еј•е…ҘжқӮиҙЁпјҲеҰӮFeжұЎжҹ“пјү
жә¶ж¶Іжі•йў—зІ’еқҮеҢҖпјҢйҖӮеҗҲи§„жЁЎеҢ–йңҖй«ҳжё©еҺ»йҷӨжә¶еүӮж®Ӣз•ҷ
еӣәзӣёеҸҚеә”й«ҳзәҜеәҰпјҢйҖӮз”ЁдәҺеӨҚжқӮжҲҗеҲҶиғҪиҖ—й«ҳпјҢйңҖй«ҳжё©зғ§з»“

жңӘжқҘз ”з©¶ж–№еҗ‘

  • ж–°жқҗж–ҷ
    • жӣҙй«ҳзҰ»еӯҗдј еҜјзҺҮ
    • жӣҙеҘҪзҡ„жңәжў°жҖ§иғҪ
  • еҲ¶еӨҮе·ҘиүәдјҳеҢ–
  • дјҳеҢ–з”өи§ЈиҙЁз•Ңйқў

Li-argyroditeзұ»еһӢвҖ”вҖ”Li6PS5X(X=Cl, Br)

з”өеӣәжҖҒз”өи§ЈиҙЁе·ҘдҪңеҺҹзҗҶ

  • жү©ж•ЈжңәеҲ¶пјҡ

    • з©әдҪҚжңәеҲ¶пјҡжҷ¶дҪ“з»“жһ„дёӯеӯҳеңЁдёҖдәӣз©әдҪҚпјҲеҚіжҷ¶ж јдёӯжң¬еә”еӯҳеңЁзҰ»еӯҗзҡ„дҪҚзҪ®еҚҙз©әзјәпјүпјҢй”ӮзҰ»еӯҗеҸҜд»ҘйҖҡиҝҮи·іи·ғеҲ°зӣёйӮ»зҡ„з©әдҪҚжқҘе®һзҺ°жү©ж•ЈгҖӮеҪ“жё©еәҰеҚҮй«ҳж—¶пјҢзҰ»еӯҗзҡ„зғӯиҝҗеҠЁеҠ еү§пјҢй”ӮзҰ»еӯҗиҺ·еҫ—и¶іеӨҹзҡ„иғҪйҮҸе…ӢжңҚе‘ЁеӣҙзҰ»еӯҗзҡ„жқҹзјҡпјҢд»ҺиҖҢжӣҙе®№жҳ“и·іи·ғеҲ°з©әдҪҚдёӯгҖӮ
    • й—ҙйҡҷжңәеҲ¶пјҡжңүдәӣй”ӮзҰ»еӯҗе№¶дёҚеҚ жҚ®жӯЈеёёзҡ„жҷ¶ж јдҪҚзҪ®пјҢиҖҢжҳҜеӨ„дәҺжҷ¶ж јзҡ„й—ҙйҡҷдҪҚзҪ®гҖӮиҝҷдәӣй—ҙйҡҷй”ӮзҰ»еӯҗеҸҜд»ҘеңЁжҷ¶ж јзҡ„й—ҙйҡҷдёӯз§»еҠЁпјҢд»ҺдёҖдёӘй—ҙйҡҷдҪҚзҪ®и·іи·ғеҲ°еҸҰдёҖдёӘй—ҙйҡҷдҪҚзҪ®пјҢд»ҺиҖҢе®һзҺ°жү©ж•ЈгҖӮ

  • еҮҸе°Ҹжү©ж•ЈиғҪеһ’пјҡжҺәжқӮ

    • ж”№еҸҳжҷ¶дҪ“з»“жһ„
    • ж”№еҸҳеҢ–еӯҰй”®
    • еўһеҠ Li+жө“еәҰ

J. Mater. Chem. A, 2024, 12, 27011

дјҳеҠҝ

  • е…ЁеӣәжҖҒй”Ӯз”өжұ пјҲASSLBsпјүеӣ еӣәжҖҒз”өи§ЈиҙЁзҡ„дҪҝз”ЁпјҢеңЁиҝҪжұӮй«ҳиғҪйҮҸеҜҶеәҰе’ҢеҸҜйқ еӮЁиғҪж–№йқўе…·жңүйҮҚиҰҒж„Ҹд№үx
  • зЎ«еҢ–зү©еӣәжҖҒз”өи§ЈиҙЁзҡ„жҷ¶дҪ“з»“жһ„йҖҡеёёе…·жңүзӣёеҜ№ејҖж”ҫзҡ„жЎҶжһ¶пјҢеӯҳеңЁиҫғеӨҡзҡ„зҰ»еӯҗйҖҡйҒ“гҖӮзЎ«пјҲSпјүзҰ»еӯҗеҚҠеҫ„иҫғеӨ§пјҢдёҺй”ӮзҰ»еӯҗд№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„зӣёдә’дҪңз”ЁиҫғејұпјҢдҪҝеҫ—й”ӮзҰ»еӯҗеңЁе…¶дёӯиҝҒз§»ж—¶еҸ—еҲ°зҡ„йҳ»зўҚиҫғе°ҸпјҢиғҪеӨҹжҜ”иҫғе®№жҳ“ең°еңЁжҷ¶ж јдёӯз§»еҠЁ
  • зЎ«еҢ–зү©зҡ„еҢ–еӯҰй”®жҖ§иҙЁзӣёеҜ№иҫғејұпјҢеңЁеҸ—еҲ°еӨ–еҠӣдҪңз”Ёж—¶пјҢе…¶еҺҹеӯҗжҲ–зҰ»еӯҗиғҪеӨҹзӣёеҜ№е®№жҳ“ең°еҸ‘з”ҹдҪҚзҪ®и°ғж•ҙе’ҢйҮҚжҺ’пјҢдҪҝеҫ—жқҗж–ҷиғҪеӨҹеҸ‘з”ҹдёҖе®ҡзЁӢеәҰзҡ„еҪўеҸҳиҖҢдёҚз ҙиЈӮпјҢиЎЁзҺ°еҮәиҫғеҘҪзҡ„е»¶еұ•жҖ§

и§ЈеҶій—®йўҳ

  • зЎ«еҢ–зү©еӣәжҖҒз”өи§ЈиҙЁпјҲSSEsпјүеӣ е®Өжё©й”ӮзҰ»еӯҗдј еҜјзҺҮй«ҳе’Ңе»¶еұ•жҖ§еҘҪиҖҢе…·жңүз«һдәүеҠӣпјҢдҪҶеӯҳеңЁдёҺй”ӮйҮ‘еұһз•ҢйқўеҸҚеә”дёҘйҮҚд»ҘеҸҠеҜ№ж°ҙеҲҶжһҒеәҰж•Ҹж„ҹдёӨдёӘз“¶йўҲй—®йўҳ
    • з•ҢйқўеҸҚеә”пјҡзҹҝзү©дёӯзҡ„й«ҳд»·йҮ‘еұһеҰӮGeгҖҒSnдјҡдёҺLiеҸҚеә”пјҢеңЁLi/SSEз•ҢйқўеҪўжҲҗж··еҗҲзҰ»еӯҗ - з”өеӯҗдј еҜјзҡ„дёӯй—ҙзӣёпјҢиҝҷдјҡеҜјиҮҙSSEзҡ„жҢҒз»ӯйҷҚи§Је’Ңз•Ңйқўйҳ»жҠ—еўһеҠ 
      • жҺәжқӮеҗҺпјҢеңЁLi/LPSC - 0.05з•ҢйқўдјҡеҺҹдҪҚеҪўжҲҗй«ҳеәҰзҰ»еӯҗдј еҜјдҪҶз”өеӯҗз»қзјҳзҡ„LiI
      • еңЁLi/LPSC - 0.05з•ҢйқўиҝҳдјҡеҪўжҲҗйҖӮйҮҸзҡ„Li - Snе’ҢLi - SbеҗҲйҮ‘
      • йҷҚдҪҺдәҶLiвҒәжү©ж•ЈеҠҝеһ’пјҢеҮҸе°‘дәҶз•ҢйқўеӨ„зҡ„жһҒеҢ–е’ҢеүҜеҸҚеә”
    • еҜ№ж°ҙж•Ҹж„ҹпјҡSSEsдёӯзҡ„дёҖдәӣжҲҗеҲҶпјҲеҰӮзЎ«д»ЈзЈ·й…ёзӣҗзӯүпјүж°ҙи§Ј
      • HSABзҗҶи®әпјҢеҪўжҲҗдәҶзЁіе®ҡзҡ„пјҲP,Sn,SbпјүSвӮ„еҚ•е…ғпјҢе…·жңүејәзҡ„Sn - Sе’ҢSb - Sй”®

жқҗж–ҷ

  • жҖқи·ҜпјҡйҖҡиҝҮжҺәжқӮжқҘж”№иҝӣжҖ§иғҪиүҜеҘҪдҪҶзЁіе®ҡжҖ§дёҚеӨҹеҘҪзҡ„з”өи§ЈиҙЁ
    • зЈ·еҹәзЎ«й“¶й”—зҹҝLi6PS5Cl(LPSC)+й”‘еҹәзЎ«й“¶й”—зҹҝLi6.4Sn0.4Sb0.6S5I(LSSSI)вҖ”> LSSSIжҺәжқӮзҡ„LSPCз”өи§ЈиҙЁ(LPSC - x, 0.0 вүӨ x вүӨ 0.1)
  • з”өжұ 
    • жӯЈжһҒвҖ”вҖ”Li2OеҢ…иҰҶзҡ„LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2пјҲNCM811@Li2Oпјү
    • иҙҹжһҒвҖ”вҖ”Li
    • з”өи§ЈиҙЁпјҡLSSSIжҺәжқӮзҡ„LPSC - xзі»еҲ—з”өи§ЈиҙЁпјҢе…¶дёӯLPSC - 0.05иЎЁзҺ°еҮәжңҖдјҳжҖ§иғҪ

е…ій”®еҸӮж•°

  • зҰ»еӯҗдј еҜјзҺҮ
  • дёҙз•Ңз”өжөҒеҜҶеәҰCCD
  • з”өжұ жҖ§иғҪеҸӮж•°

Q&A

  1. зЎ«й“¶й”—зҹҝз”өи§ЈиҙЁзҡ„йҖҡејҸдёә LiвӮҶPSвӮ…XпјҲX=Cl, Br, IпјүпјҢи§ЈйҮҠе…¶жҷ¶дҪ“з»“жһ„дёӯ PSвӮ„ВівҒ»еӣӣйқўдҪ“ е’Ң еҚӨзҙ зҰ»еӯҗпјҲXвҒ»пјү зҡ„дҪңз”ЁгҖӮдёәд»Җд№ҲеҚӨзҙ з§Қзұ»дјҡеҪұе“ҚзҰ»еӯҗз”өеҜјзҺҮпјҹ
    1. PSвӮ„ВівҒ»еӣӣйқўдҪ“пјҡжһ„жҲҗзЎ«й“¶й”—зҹҝзҡ„еҲҡжҖ§йӘЁжһ¶пјҢжҸҗдҫӣзЁіе®ҡзҡ„дёүз»ҙз»“жһ„жЎҶжһ¶пјҢе…Ғи®ёLiвҒәеңЁй—ҙйҡҷдҪҚзӮ№иҝҒз§»гҖӮ
    2. еҚӨзҙ зҰ»еӯҗпјҲXвҒ»пјүпјҡеҚ жҚ®зЎ«з©әдҪҚпјҢи°ғиҠӮжҷ¶ж јеҸӮж•°пјҲеҰӮLiвӮҶPSвӮ…Clжҷ¶иғһеҸӮж•° ~10.0 Г…пјүпјҢжү©еӨ§LiвҒәиҝҒз§»йҖҡйҒ“гҖӮ
    3. еҚӨзҙ еҪұе“ҚпјҡClвҒ»пјҲиҫғе°ҸпјүдҪҝжҷ¶ж јзҙ§еҜҶпјҢLiвҒәиҝҒз§»и·Ҝеҫ„еҸ—йҷҗпјӣIвҒ»пјҲиҫғеӨ§пјүжү©еӨ§йҖҡйҒ“пјҢз”өеҜјзҺҮжҸҗй«ҳпјҲLiвӮҶPSвӮ…Iиҫҫ ~3 mS/cmпјү
  2. ж–ҮзҢ®жҸҗеҲ°зЎ«й“¶й”—зҹҝз”өи§ЈиҙЁзҡ„LiвҒәиҝҒз§»жҙ»еҢ–иғҪиҫғдҪҺпјҲ~0.2 eVпјүпјҢиҜ•д»ҺеҚҸеҗҢиҝҒз§»пјҲcooperative migrationпјү зҡ„и§’еәҰи§ЈйҮҠе…¶й«ҳзҰ»еӯҗз”өеҜјзҺҮзҡ„жқҘжәҗ
    1. еҚҸеҗҢиҝҒз§»пјҡеӨҡдёӘLiвҒәйҖҡиҝҮе…ұдә«дҪҚзӮ№еҚҸеҗҢи·іи·ғпјҢйҷҚдҪҺеҚ•дёӘLi+иҝҒз§»зҡ„жҙ»еҢ–иғҪпјҲLiвӮҶPSвӮ…Clжҙ»еҢ–иғҪ ~0.22 eVпјү
    2. еҚӨзҙ дҪңз”ЁпјҡXвҒ»жһҒеҢ–дҪңз”ЁеүҠејұLiвҒәдёҺйӘЁжһ¶зҡ„зӣёдә’дҪңз”ЁпјҢдҝғиҝӣLi+и„ұеөҢпјҲеҰӮLiвӮҶPSвӮ…BrдёӯBrвҒ»зҡ„жһҒеҢ–ж•Ҳеә”ејәдәҺClвҒ»пјү
  3. иӢҘеңЁLiвӮҶPSвӮ…ClдёӯжҺәжқӮ Sn е’Ң OпјҲеҰӮLiвӮҶ.вӮғPвӮҖ.вӮҮSnвӮҖ.вӮғSвӮ„.вӮ„OвӮҖ.вӮғIпјүпјҢиҝҷз§ҚвҖңеҸҢжҺәжқӮвҖқеҰӮдҪ•еҗҢж—¶жҸҗеҚҮзҰ»еӯҗз”өеҜјзҺҮе’Ңз©әж°”зЁіе®ҡжҖ§пјҹ
    1. SnжҺәжқӮпјҡSnвҒҙвҒәеҸ–д»ЈPвҒөвҒәпјҢеј•е…ҘйҳізҰ»еӯҗз©әдҪҚпјҢжҸҗеҚҮLiвҒәжө“еәҰпјҲеҰӮLiвӮҶ.вӮғPвӮҖ.вӮҮSnвӮҖ.вӮғSвӮ„.вӮ„OвӮҖ.вӮғIз”өеҜјзҺҮ ~0.2 mS/cmпјүгҖӮ
    2. OжҺәжқӮпјҡOВІвҒ»й’қеҢ–жҷ¶з•ҢпјҢжҠ‘еҲ¶HвӮӮOеҗёйҷ„пјҲз©әж°”зЁіе®ҡжҖ§жҸҗеҚҮиҮі >24е°Ҹж—¶@30% RHпјүгҖӮ

entry.title: е…ідәҺз©әдҪҚгҖҒз©әйҡҷгҖҒй—ҙйҡҷзҡ„з®ҖеҚ•и®Ёи®ә
entry.content:
зҰ»еӯҗжҺәжқӮеҸҜеҪўжҲҗз©әдҪҚ(vacancy)

1. йҳізҰ»еӯҗз©әдҪҚзҡ„дә§з”ҹжңәеҲ¶

йҳізҰ»еӯҗз©әдҪҚпјҲеҚійҳізҰ»еӯҗдәҡжҷ¶ж јдёӯзҡ„з©әзјәпјүйҖҡеёёз”ұй«ҳд»·йҳізҰ»еӯҗеҸ–д»ЈжҲ–дҪҺд»·йҳҙзҰ»еӯҗеҸ–д»Јеј•иө·пјҢйҖҡиҝҮз”өиҚ·иЎҘеҒҝе®һзҺ°з”өдёӯжҖ§гҖӮ

(1) й«ҳд»·йҳізҰ»еӯҗжҺәжқӮ вҶ’ дә§з”ҹйҳізҰ»еӯҗз©әдҪҚ

зӨәдҫӢпјҡеңЁзҹіжҰҙзҹіеһӢз”өи§ЈиҙЁLi7La3Zr2O12\rm{Li_7La_3Zr_2O_{12}}Li7вҖӢLa3вҖӢZr2вҖӢO12вҖӢпјҲLLZOпјүдёӯпјҢAlВівҒәеҸ–д»ЈLiвҒәпјҲLiвҒәдёә+1д»·пјүпјҡ

  • жҜҸдёӘAlВівҒәеҸ–д»ЈLiвҒәж—¶пјҢеј•е…Ҙ**+2йўқеӨ–жӯЈз”өиҚ·**пјҲAlВівҒә - LiвҒә = +2пјүгҖӮ
  • дёәз»ҙжҢҒз”өдёӯжҖ§пјҢйңҖз§»йҷӨ2дёӘLiвҒәпјҢеҪўжҲҗ2дёӘLiвҒәз©әдҪҚпјҲеҢ–еӯҰејҸеҸҳдёәLi7вҲ’2xAlxLa3Zr2O12\rm{Li_{7-2x}Al_xLa_3Zr_2O_{12}}Li7вҲ’2xвҖӢAlxвҖӢLa3вҖӢZr2вҖӢO12вҖӢпјүгҖӮ
  • ж•ҲжһңпјҡLiвҒәз©әдҪҚжө“еәҰеўһеҠ пјҢзҰ»еӯҗз”өеҜјзҺҮжҸҗеҚҮ

(2) дҪҺд»·йҳҙзҰ»еӯҗжҺәжқӮ вҶ’ дә§з”ҹйҳізҰ»еӯҗз©әдҪҚ

зӨәдҫӢпјҡзЎ«й“¶й”—зҹҝLiвӮҶPSвӮ…ClдёӯпјҢClвҒ»пјҲ-1д»·пјүеҸ–д»ЈSВІвҒ»пјҲ-2д»·пјүпјҡ

  • жҜҸдёӘClвҒ»еҸ–д»ЈSВІвҒ»ж—¶пјҢеҮҸе°‘1дёӘиҙҹз”өиҚ·пјҲClвҒ» - SВІвҒ» = +1пјүгҖӮ
  • дёәз»ҙжҢҒз”өдёӯжҖ§пјҢйңҖеҮҸе°‘1дёӘLiвҒәпјҲLiвҒәдёә+1д»·пјүпјҢеҪўжҲҗLiвҒәз©әдҪҚпјҲеҢ–еӯҰејҸеҸҳдёәLiвӮҶвӮӢвӮ“PSвӮ…вӮӢвӮ“ClвӮҒвӮҠвӮ“пјүгҖӮ
  • е®һйӘҢиҜҒжҚ®пјҡLiвӮ….вӮ…PSвӮ„.вӮ…ClвӮҒ.вӮ…дёӯLiз©әдҪҚеҚ жҜ”жҸҗй«ҳпјҢзҰ»еӯҗз”өеҜјзҺҮжҳҫи‘—еўһеҠ пјҲд»ҺLiвӮҶPSвӮ…Clзҡ„3 mS/cmжҸҗеҚҮиҮі12 mS/cmпјүгҖӮ

2. йҳҙзҰ»еӯҗз©әдҪҚзҡ„дә§з”ҹжңәеҲ¶

йҳҙзҰ»еӯҗз©әдҪҚпјҲйҳҙзҰ»еӯҗдәҡжҷ¶ж јдёӯзҡ„з©әзјәпјүйҖҡеёёз”ұй«ҳд»·йҳҙзҰ»еӯҗжҺәжқӮжҲ–йҳізҰ»еӯҗз©әдҪҚиЎҘеҒҝеј•иө·гҖӮ

(1) й«ҳд»·йҳҙзҰ»еӯҗжҺәжқӮ вҶ’ дә§з”ҹйҳҙзҰ»еӯҗз©әдҪҚ

зӨәдҫӢпјҡеңЁж°§еҢ–й”ҶпјҲZrOвӮӮпјүдёӯжҺәжқӮYВівҒәпјҲеҸ–д»ЈZrвҒҙвҒәпјүпјҡ

  • жҜҸдёӘYВівҒәеҸ–д»ЈZrвҒҙвҒәж—¶пјҢеј•е…Ҙ**-1з”өиҚ·зјәйҷ·**пјҲYВівҒә - ZrвҒҙвҒә = -1пјүгҖӮ
  • дёәз»ҙжҢҒз”өдёӯжҖ§пјҢйңҖдә§з”ҹж°§з©әдҪҚпјҲжҜҸдёӘOВІвҒ»з©әзјәиҙЎзҢ®+2з”өиҚ·пјүпјҢеҢ–еӯҰејҸдёәYвӮ“ZrвӮҒвӮӢвӮ“OвӮӮвӮӢвӮ“/вӮӮгҖӮ
  • ж•Ҳжһңпјҡж°§з©әдҪҚдҝғиҝӣOВІвҒ»иҝҒз§»пјҲеҰӮй’ҮзЁіе®ҡж°§еҢ–й”ҶYSZз”ЁдәҺзҮғж–ҷз”өжұ пјүгҖӮ

(2) йҳізҰ»еӯҗз©әдҪҚиЎҘеҒҝ вҶ’ дә§з”ҹйҳҙзҰ»еӯҗз©әдҪҚ

зӨәдҫӢпјҡеңЁй’ҷй’ӣзҹҝеһӢж°§еҢ–зү©дёӯпјҢSrВІвҒәеҸ–д»ЈLaВівҒәпјҲLaвӮҒвӮӢвӮ“SrвӮ“MnOвӮғпјүпјҡ

  • жҜҸдёӘSrВІвҒәеҸ–д»ЈLaВівҒәж—¶пјҢеј•е…Ҙ**-1з”өиҚ·зјәйҷ·**пјҲSrВІвҒә - LaВівҒә = -1пјүгҖӮ
  • иЎҘеҒҝж–№ејҸеҢ…жӢ¬пјҡ
    • йҳізҰ»еӯҗз©әдҪҚпјҲеҰӮLaВівҒәз©әзјәпјҢиҙЎзҢ®+3з”өиҚ·пјүпјӣ
    • йҳҙзҰ»еӯҗз©әдҪҚпјҲеҰӮOВІвҒ»з©әзјәпјҢиҙЎзҢ®+2з”өиҚ·пјүгҖӮ
  • е®һйҷ…дҪ“зі»дёӯеёёж··еҗҲдёӨз§ҚиЎҘеҒҝжңәеҲ¶гҖӮ

3. еҝ«йҖҹеҲӨж–ӯжҺәжқӮдёҺз©әдҪҚе…ізі»зҡ„жі•еҲҷ

(1) з”өиҚ·е®ҲжҒ’жі•еҲҷ

  • жҖ»з”өиҚ·е№іиЎЎпјҡеҸ–д»ЈзҰ»еӯҗдёҺеҺҹзҰ»еӯҗзҡ„д»·жҖҒе·®йңҖйҖҡиҝҮз©әдҪҚжҲ–й—ҙйҡҷиЎҘеҒҝгҖӮ
  • з»ҸйӘҢе…¬ејҸпјҡ

О”q=zжҺәжқӮвҲ’zеҺҹе§Ӣ=вҲ‘(з©әдҪҚжҲ–й—ҙйҡҷз”өиҚ·иҙЎзҢ®)\Delta q = z_{\text{жҺәжқӮ}} - z_{\text{еҺҹе§Ӣ}} = \sum (\text{з©әдҪҚжҲ–й—ҙйҡҷз”өиҚ·иҙЎзҢ®})О”q=zжҺәжқӮвҖӢвҲ’zеҺҹе§ӢвҖӢ=вҲ‘(з©әдҪҚжҲ–й—ҙйҡҷз”өиҚ·иҙЎзҢ®)

(2) д»·жҖҒе·®дёҺз©әдҪҚз¬ҰеҸ·е…ізі»

жҺәжқӮзұ»еһӢд»·жҖҒе·®пјҲО”qпјүиЎҘеҒҝж–№ејҸз©әдҪҚзұ»еһӢ
й«ҳд»·йҳізҰ»еӯҗвҶ’дҪҺд»·дҪҚзӮ№О”q > 0дә§з”ҹйҳізҰ»еӯҗз©әдҪҚйҳізҰ»еӯҗз©әдҪҚ
дҪҺд»·йҳізҰ»еӯҗвҶ’й«ҳд»·дҪҚзӮ№О”q < 0дә§з”ҹйҳізҰ»еӯҗй—ҙйҡҷжҲ–йҳҙзҰ»еӯҗз©әдҪҚйҳҙзҰ»еӯҗз©әдҪҚ
й«ҳд»·йҳҙзҰ»еӯҗвҶ’дҪҺд»·дҪҚзӮ№О”q > 0дә§з”ҹйҳҙзҰ»еӯҗз©әдҪҚйҳҙзҰ»еӯҗз©әдҪҚ
дҪҺд»·йҳҙзҰ»еӯҗвҶ’й«ҳд»·дҪҚзӮ№О”q < 0дә§з”ҹйҳҙзҰ»еӯҗй—ҙйҡҷжҲ–йҳізҰ»еӯҗз©әдҪҚйҳізҰ»еӯҗз©әдҪҚ

4. е…ёеһӢжқҗж–ҷдҪ“зі»еҜ№жҜ”

жқҗж–ҷдҪ“зі»жҺәжқӮзұ»еһӢз©әдҪҚзұ»еһӢз”өеҜјзҺҮжҸҗеҚҮжңәеҲ¶
LiвӮ….вӮ…PSвӮ„.вӮ…ClвӮҒ.вӮ…ClвҒ»еҸ–д»ЈSВІвҒ»LiвҒәз©әдҪҚйҷҚдҪҺLiвҒәиҝҒз§»иғҪеһ’
LLZOпјҲAlВівҒәжҺәжқӮпјүAlВівҒәеҸ–д»ЈLiвҒәLiвҒәз©әдҪҚеўһеҠ LiвҒәиҝҒз§»йҖҡйҒ“
YSZпјҲYВівҒәжҺәжқӮпјүYВівҒәеҸ–д»ЈZrвҒҙвҒәOВІвҒ»з©әдҪҚдҝғиҝӣOВІвҒ»зҰ»еӯҗиҝҒз§»
LaвӮҖ.вӮҲSrвӮҖ.вӮӮMnOвӮғSrВІвҒәеҸ–д»ЈLaВівҒәOВІвҒ»з©әдҪҚдјҳеҢ–ж°§зҰ»еӯҗдј иҫ“и·Ҝеҫ„

5. жҖ»з»“дёҺи®°еҝҶжҠҖе·§

  1. вҖңй«ҳд»·еҸ–д»ЈвҶ’дә§з”ҹеҗҢзұ»еһӢз©әдҪҚвҖқпјҡ

    • й«ҳд»·йҳізҰ»еӯҗеҸ–д»ЈдҪҺд»·йҳізҰ»еӯҗ вҶ’ йҳізҰ»еӯҗз©әдҪҚпјҲеҰӮAlВівҒәвҶ’LiвҒәпјүгҖӮ
    • й«ҳд»·йҳҙзҰ»еӯҗеҸ–д»ЈдҪҺд»·йҳҙзҰ»еӯҗ вҶ’ йҳҙзҰ»еӯҗз©әдҪҚпјҲеҰӮClвҒ»вҶ’SВІвҒ»йңҖз»“еҗҲз”өиҚ·иЎҘеҒҝпјүгҖӮ

  2. вҖңдҪҺд»·еҸ–д»ЈвҶ’иЎҘеҒҝж–№ејҸеӨҡж ·вҖқпјҡ

    • дҪҺд»·йҳізҰ»еӯҗеҸ–д»Јй«ҳд»·йҳізҰ»еӯҗ вҶ’ йҳҙзҰ»еӯҗз©әдҪҚжҲ–йҳізҰ»еӯҗй—ҙйҡҷгҖӮ
    • дҪҺд»·йҳҙзҰ»еӯҗеҸ–д»Јй«ҳд»·йҳҙзҰ»еӯҗ вҶ’ йҳізҰ»еӯҗз©әдҪҚжҲ–йҳҙзҰ»еӯҗй—ҙйҡҷгҖӮ

  3. е®һжҲҳйӘҢиҜҒпјҡ
    йҖҡиҝҮXе°„зәҝиЎҚе°„пјҲXRDпјүзІҫдҝ®зЎ®е®ҡз©әдҪҚжө“еәҰпјҢжҲ–з»“еҗҲеӣәжҖҒж ёзЈҒпјҲеҰӮвҒ·Li MAS NMRпјүеҲҶжһҗеұҖеҹҹLiвҒәзҺҜеўғеҸҳеҢ–гҖӮ

еңЁжқҗж–ҷз§‘еӯҰдёӯпјҢвҖңз©әдҪҚвҖқпјҲVacancyпјүгҖҒвҖңй—ҙйҡҷвҖқпјҲInterstitialпјүе’ҢвҖңз©әйҡҷвҖқпјҲеӯ”жҙһ/VoidпјүжҳҜжҸҸиҝ°жҷ¶дҪ“зјәйҷ·зҡ„дёҚеҗҢжҰӮеҝөпјҢе®ғд»¬еҜ№жқҗж–ҷжҖ§иғҪзҡ„еҪұе“ҚжҲӘз„¶дёҚеҗҢгҖӮд»ҘдёӢиҜҰз»ҶеҢәеҲҶиҝҷдёүиҖ…пјҢе№¶з»“еҗҲзЎ«й“¶й”—зҹҝз”өи§ЈиҙЁзҡ„дҫӢеӯҗиҜҙжҳҺе…¶дҪңз”Ёпјҡ

еҜ№VacancyгҖҒInterstitialгҖҒVoidзҡ„з®ҖеҚ•иҫЁжһҗ

1. з©әдҪҚпјҲVacancyпјү

  • е®ҡд№үпјҡжҷ¶ж јдёӯжң¬еә”жңүеҺҹеӯҗ/зҰ»еӯҗеҚ жҚ®зҡ„дҪҚзҪ®зјәеӨұпјҢеҪўжҲҗзҡ„зӮ№зјәйҷ·гҖӮ
  • еҪўжҲҗеҺҹеӣ пјҡ
    • зғӯжҢҜеҠЁпјҲжүҖжңүжқҗж–ҷеңЁйқһз»қеҜ№йӣ¶еәҰдёӢеқҮеӯҳеңЁпјүпјӣ
    • жҺәжқӮеҜјиҮҙзҡ„з”өиҚ·иЎҘеҒҝпјҲеҰӮClвҒ»еҸ–д»ЈSВІвҒ»ж—¶дә§з”ҹLiвҒәз©әдҪҚпјүгҖӮ
  • еҜ№жҖ§иғҪзҡ„еҪұе“Қпјҡ
    • жӯЈйқўпјҡз©әдҪҚеҸҜдёәзҰ»еӯҗиҝҒз§»жҸҗдҫӣи·Ҝеҫ„пјҢжҸҗеҚҮзҰ»еӯҗз”өеҜјзҺҮпјҲеҰӮLiвҒәз©әдҪҚдҝғиҝӣдј еҜјпјүпјӣ
    • иҙҹйқўпјҡиҝҮйҮҸз©әдҪҚеҸҜиғҪйҷҚдҪҺз»“жһ„зЁіе®ҡжҖ§гҖӮ
  • зӨәдҫӢпјҡ
    еңЁLiвӮ….вӮ…PSвӮ„.вӮ…ClвӮҒ.вӮ…дёӯпјҢClвҒ»еҸ–д»ЈSВІвҒ»еҜјиҮҙLiвҒәз©әдҪҚпјҢжҳҫи‘—жҸҗеҚҮй”ӮзҰ»еӯҗз”өеҜјзҺҮгҖӮ

2. й—ҙйҡҷпјҲInterstitialпјү

  • е®ҡд№үпјҡеҺҹеӯҗ/зҰ»еӯҗеҚ жҚ®жҷ¶ж јжӯЈеёёдҪҚзҪ®д№ӢеӨ–зҡ„й—ҙйҡҷдҪҚзҪ®пјҢеҪўжҲҗзҡ„зӮ№зјәйҷ·гҖӮ
  • еҪўжҲҗеҺҹеӣ пјҡ
    • е°Ҹе°әеҜёеҺҹеӯҗпјҲеҰӮLiвҒәгҖҒHвҒәпјүжҳ“иҝӣе…Ҙй—ҙйҡҷпјӣ
    • жҺәжқӮжҲ–йқһеҢ–еӯҰи®ЎйҮҸжҜ”и®ҫи®ЎпјҲеҰӮиҝҮйҮҸLiеј•е…Ҙй—ҙйҡҷLiвҒәпјүгҖӮ
  • еҜ№жҖ§иғҪзҡ„еҪұе“Қпјҡ
    • жӯЈйқўпјҡй—ҙйҡҷзҰ»еӯҗеҸҜдҪңдёәйўқеӨ–иҪҪжөҒеӯҗпјҲеҰӮLiвӮҒвӮҖGePвӮӮSвӮҒвӮӮдёӯзҡ„й—ҙйҡҷLiвҒәжҸҗеҚҮз”өеҜјзҺҮпјүпјӣ
    • иҙҹйқўпјҡеҸҜиғҪйҳ»еЎһиҝҒз§»йҖҡйҒ“жҲ–еј•еҸ‘жҷ¶ж јз•ёеҸҳгҖӮ
  • зӨәдҫӢпјҡ
    еңЁLi-richжқҗж–ҷдёӯпјҢй—ҙйҡҷLiвҒәеҸҜдёҺз©әдҪҚеҚҸеҗҢеҪўжҲҗвҖңеҚҸеҗҢиҝҒз§»жңәеҲ¶вҖқпјҢиҝӣдёҖжӯҘжҸҗеҚҮз”өеҜјзҺҮгҖӮ

3. з©әйҡҷпјҲеӯ”жҙһ/Voidпјү

  • е®ҡд№үпјҡжқҗж–ҷдёӯжңӘиў«еҺҹеӯҗ/зҰ»еӯҗеҚ жҚ®зҡ„е®Ҹи§ӮжҲ–д»Ӣи§Ӯеӯ”жҙһпјҢеұһдәҺдҪ“з§Ҝзјәйҷ·гҖӮ
  • еҪўжҲҗеҺҹеӣ пјҡ
    • еҗҲжҲҗе·Ҙиүәзјәйҷ·пјҲеҰӮзғ§з»“дёҚе……еҲҶеҜјиҮҙеӯ”йҡҷпјүпјӣ
    • е…ғзҙ жҢҘеҸ‘жҲ–зӣёеҲҶзҰ»пјҲеҰӮзЎ«еҢ–зү©з”өи§ЈиҙЁй«ҳжё©зғ§з»“ж—¶зЎ«жҚҹеӨұпјүгҖӮ
  • еҜ№жҖ§иғҪзҡ„еҪұе“Қпјҡ
    • иҙҹйқўпјҡ
      • йҷҚдҪҺжқҗж–ҷиҮҙеҜҶеәҰпјҢеўһеҠ жҷ¶з•Ңз”өйҳ»пјӣ
      • еӯ”жҙһеӨ„жҳ“жҲҗдёәжһқжҷ¶з”ҹй•ҝзҡ„иө·зӮ№пјҢеј•еҸ‘з”өжұ зҹӯи·ҜгҖӮ
    • жӯЈйқўпјҡ
      еҸҜжҺ§еӯ”йҡҷзҺҮеҸҜз”ЁдәҺеӨҚеҗҲз”өи§ЈиҙЁи®ҫи®ЎпјҲеҰӮиҒҡеҗҲзү©еЎ«е……еӯ”жҙһж”№е–„з•ҢйқўжҺҘи§ҰпјүгҖӮ
  • зӨәдҫӢпјҡ
    дј з»ҹзғ§з»“зҡ„LiвӮҶPSвӮ…ClиҮҙеҜҶеәҰд»…~85%пјҢиҖҢж”ҫз”өзӯүзҰ»еӯҗзғ§з»“пјҲSPSпјүеҸҜе°Ҷе…¶жҸҗеҚҮиҮі95%пјҢжҳҫи‘—йҷҚдҪҺеӯ”жҙһжҜ”дҫӢгҖӮ

4. дёүиҖ…зҡ„ж ёеҝғеҢәеҲ«жҖ»з»“

зү№еҫҒз©әдҪҚпјҲVacancyпјүй—ҙйҡҷпјҲInterstitialпјүз©әйҡҷпјҲVoidпјү
зјәйҷ·зұ»еһӢзӮ№зјәйҷ·зӮ№зјәйҷ·дҪ“з§Ҝзјәйҷ·
е°әеәҰеҺҹеӯҗзә§еҲ«пјҲГ…е°әеәҰпјүеҺҹеӯҗзә§еҲ«пјҲГ…е°әеәҰпјүеҫ®и§ӮиҮіе®Ҹи§ӮпјҲnm~Ојmпјү
еҜ№дј еҜјзҡ„еҪұе“ҚжҸҗдҫӣиҝҒз§»и·Ҝеҫ„пјҲе…ій”®пјүеҸҜиғҪиҫ…еҠ©жҲ–йҳ»зўҚдј еҜјйҳ»зўҚдј еҜјпјҲеўһеҠ з”өйҳ»пјү
еҸҜжҺ§жҖ§еҸҜйҖҡиҝҮжҺәжқӮзІҫзЎ®и°ғжҺ§йңҖи°ғиҠӮжҲҗеҲҶжҲ–еҗҲжҲҗжқЎд»¶дҫқиө–еҲ¶еӨҮе·ҘиүәдјҳеҢ–
е…ёеһӢзӨәдҫӢLiвӮ….вӮ…PSвӮ„.вӮ…ClвӮҒ.вӮ…дёӯLiвҒәз©әдҪҚLiвӮҒвӮҖGePвӮӮSвӮҒвӮӮдёӯй—ҙйҡҷLiвҒәзғ§з»“дёҚи¶ізҡ„з”өи§ЈиҙЁеӯ”жҙһ

5. еңЁзЎ«й“¶й”—зҹҝз”өи§ЈиҙЁдёӯзҡ„е®һйҷ…ж„Ҹд№ү

  • з©әдҪҚзҡ„еҝ…иҰҒжҖ§пјҡClвҒ»жҺәжқӮеј•е…Ҙзҡ„LiвҒәз©әдҪҚжҳҜжҸҗеҚҮзҰ»еӯҗз”өеҜјзҺҮзҡ„ж ёеҝғжңәеҲ¶гҖӮ
  • й—ҙйҡҷзҡ„жҪңеңЁд»·еҖјпјҡиӢҘеңЁLiвӮ….вӮ…PSвӮ„.вӮ…ClвӮҒ.вӮ…дёӯеј•е…Ҙе°‘йҮҸй—ҙйҡҷLiвҒәпјҢеҸҜиғҪиҝӣдёҖжӯҘдјҳеҢ–дј еҜји·Ҝеҫ„пјҲйңҖйҒҝе…Қйҳ»еЎһйҖҡйҒ“пјүгҖӮ
  • з©әйҡҷзҡ„и§„йҒҝпјҡйҖҡиҝҮж¶Ізӣёзғ§з»“жҲ–й«ҳеҺӢзғ§з»“еҮҸе°‘еӯ”жҙһпјҢзЎ®дҝқиҮҙеҜҶеҢ–д»ҘйҷҚдҪҺжҷ¶з•Ңйҳ»жҠ—гҖӮ

жҖ»з»“

  • з©әдҪҚ вү  й—ҙйҡҷ вү  з©әйҡҷпјҡдёүиҖ…еҲҶеұһдёҚеҗҢе°әеәҰе’Ңзұ»еһӢзҡ„зјәйҷ·пјҢдҪңз”ЁжңәеҲ¶иҝҘејӮгҖӮ
  • з ”з©¶ж–№еҗ‘пјҡ
    • еҲ©з”ЁжҺәжқӮзІҫеҮҶи°ғжҺ§з©әдҪҚжө“еәҰпјҲеҰӮClвҒ»/BrвҒ»е…ұжҺәжқӮпјүпјӣ
    • дјҳеҢ–зғ§з»“е·Ҙиүәж¶ҲйҷӨжңүе®із©әйҡҷпјӣ
    • жҺўзҙўй—ҙйҡҷзҰ»еӯҗзҡ„еҚҸеҗҢж•Ҳеә”пјҲеҰӮй«ҳзҶөи®ҫи®ЎпјүгҖӮ

еҰӮжһңйңҖиҰҒиҝӣдёҖжӯҘеҲҶжһҗзү№е®ҡжқҗж–ҷзҡ„зјәйҷ·зұ»еһӢжҲ–и®ҫи®Ўе®һйӘҢж–№жЎҲпјҢж¬ўиҝҺз»§з»ӯжҸҗй—®пјҒ

entry.title: Lesson 7 The Delights of South Island
entry.content:
The Delights of South Island

One of the odder coincidences of physical geography is the fact that there are two double islands, roughly the same size, positioned at each otherвҖҷs antipodes, or farthest-distant point. The islands of England and Ireland in the Northern Hemisphere and the islands of North Island and South Island in the southern Hemisphere are just such a coincidence. The first two islands comprise the United Kingdom and the Republic of Ireland (or Eire), and the second two islands comprise New Zealand. Among these four islands, there can be no doubt that South Island is the least polluted and most spectacularly scenic of them all.

There is much competition to make such a claim. The island of England, politically constituting England, Scotland, and Wales of the United Kingdom, is dotted with country villages set alongside rivers and lakes. There are not very tall but nonetheless rugged mountains in the north, and endless miles of rocky coastline that seem mystical. Ireland, too, is a paradise of greenery, with far fewer people than populous England and even more quaint villages scattered among its low-lying hills and forever green fields. North Island in New Zealand sports a balmy climate and the beaches to make use of it; one beach alone is more than 150 kilometers long and with relatively few people on its shores, one can pretend one is at the very end of the earth. Volcanoes, large lakes, and quickly flowing rivers traverse the land. Given the beauty of these three islands, what makes South Island so special?

Plenty. For those who like mountains, South Island is sure to please. Mt. Cook at 3764 meters is its highest peak, with 16 others above 3000 meters. Naturally, many local and foreign mountain climbers come here for the challenge of these Southern Alps. In addition, there is an extensive glacier system, endless forests, and innumerable lakes throughout this highland area. Some of the worldвҖҷs best mountain scenery is available within the 500-kilometer long chain of the Southern Alps.

Perhaps you prefer the sea? South Island is not only an island, but many tiny islets can be found off its coastline. Great deep-sea fishing, scuba diving, and snorkeling can be had, though the waters here are cooler than those of North Island. (Remember, in the Southern Hemisphere, as we go north, it gets warmer.)As fewer people live on South Island than on North Island, those who crave solitude and pristine beaches will be amazed at their luck here. With almost no heavy industry on South Island, the air, water, and land are all free of pollution. The Local seafood is therefore clean, plentiful, and never-ending.

Do healthful climates interest you? South Island is the place to be. Its temperate climate sees little snow except in the highlands and mountainous areas. Like Ireland and England, there are no extremes of temperature, either. Summers are warm, not hot, and winters are brisk rather than freezing. The fresh air is sometimes humid from the abundant rainfall of this area. Every season invites the nature lover to get out and be active in the countryside.

Of course, South Island is not for everyone. For those who need busy, crowded, noisy, and polluted cities, this Southern outpost will surely disappoint. For those who enjoy pressure and stress, South Island will leave them empty-handed. And for those who would rather stay at home or in an office in front of a computer screen or in the thumping, smoke-filled dance floors of discos, some of the worldвҖҷs best natural scenery will never entice them away. For the rest of us, though, South Island is the worldвҖҷs best kept secret. If NatureвҖҷs paradise sounds alluring, make a point of visiting South Island.

One of the odder coincidences of physical geography is the fact that there are two double islands, roughly the same size, positioned at each otherвҖҷs antipodes, or farthest-distant point. The islands of England and Ireland in the Northern Hemisphere and the islands of North Island and South Island in the southern Hemisphere are just such a coincidence. The first two islands comprise the United Kingdom and the Republic of Ireland (or Eire), and the second two islands comprise New Zealand. Among these four islands, there can be no doubt that South Island is the least polluted and most spectacularly scenic of them all.

иҮӘз„¶ең°зҗҶдёӯдёҖдёӘиҫғдёәеҘҮзү№зҡ„е·§еҗҲжҳҜпјҢдё–з•ҢдёҠеӯҳеңЁзқҖдёӨз»„еӨ§е°Ҹзӣёиҝ‘зҡ„еҸҢеІӣпјҢе®ғд»¬еҲҶеҲ«дҪҚдәҺеҪјжӯӨзҡ„еҜ№жҳ зӮ№пјҢд№ҹе°ұжҳҜең°зҗғзҡ„дёӨжһҒгҖӮеҢ—еҚҠзҗғзҡ„иӢұж је…°е’ҢзҲұе°”е…°зҫӨеІӣд»ҘеҸҠеҚ—еҚҠзҗғзҡ„ж–°иҘҝе…°еҚ—еҢ—дәҢеІӣе°ұжҳҜиҝҷж ·зҡ„е·§еҗҲгҖӮеүҚдёӨиҖ…жһ„жҲҗдәҶиҒ”еҗҲзҺӢеӣҪе’ҢзҲұе°”е…°е…ұе’ҢеӣҪпјҢеҗҺдёӨиҖ…еҲҷз»„жҲҗдәҶж–°иҘҝе…°гҖӮеңЁиҝҷеӣӣдёӘеІӣеұҝдёӯпјҢеҚ—еІӣж— з–‘жҳҜжұЎжҹ“жңҖе°‘дё”йЈҺжҷҜжңҖдёәз§ҖдёҪзҡ„гҖӮ

еҜ№дәҺи°ҒжүҚжҳҜйЈҺжҷҜжңҖз§ҖдёҪзҡ„еІӣеұҝпјҢеҗ„ж–№з«һдәүжҝҖзғҲгҖӮж”ҝжІ»дёҠжһ„жҲҗиӢұеӣҪзҡ„иӢұж је…°еІӣпјҢйҒҚеёғзқҖзӮ№зјҖеңЁжІіжөҒе’Ңж№–жіҠд№Ӣз•”зҡ„д№Ўжқ‘гҖӮеҢ—йғЁжңүиҷҪдёҚй«ҳиҖёеҚҙеҙҺеІ–зҡ„еұұи„үпјҢд»ҘеҸҠз»өе»¶ж— е°ҪгҖҒзҘһз§ҳиҺ«жөӢзҡ„еІ©зҹіжө·еІёзәҝгҖӮзҲұе°”е…°д№ҹжҳҜдёҖзүҮз»ҝж„ҸзӣҺз„¶зҡ„д№җеңҹпјҢдәәеҸЈиҝңе°‘дәҺдәәеҸЈеҜҶйӣҶзҡ„иӢұж је…°пјҢе…¶дҪҺжҙјзҡ„дёҳйҷөе’Ңз»Ҳе№ҙзҝ з»ҝзҡ„з”°йҮҺй—ҙж•ЈеёғзқҖжӣҙеӨҡеҸӨйӣ…зҡ„жқ‘иҗҪгҖӮж–°иҘҝе…°зҡ„еҢ—еІӣж°”еҖҷе®ңдәәпјҢиҝҳжңүиҜёеӨҡеҸҜдҫӣдә«еҸ—зҡ„жө·ж»©пјӣд»…дёҖдёӘжө·ж»©е°ұй•ҝиҫҫ150еӨҡе…¬йҮҢпјҢиҖҢдё”жө·еІёзәҝдёҠдәәзӣёеҜ№иҫғе°‘пјҢи®©дәәд»ҝдҪӣзҪ®иә«дәҺдё–з•Ңе°ҪеӨҙгҖӮеІӣдёҠжңүзҒ«еұұгҖҒеӨ§ж№–жіҠе’Ңж№ҚжҖҘзҡ„жІіжөҒгҖӮйүҙдәҺиҝҷдёүдёӘеІӣеұҝзҡ„зҫҺдёҪпјҢйӮЈд№ҲеҚ—еІӣз©¶з«ҹжңүдҪ•зү№еҲ«д№ӢеӨ„е‘ўпјҹ

зү№еҲ«д№ӢеӨ„еҸҜеӨҡдәҶгҖӮеҜ№дәҺе–ңзҲұеұұи„үзҡ„дәәжқҘиҜҙпјҢеҚ—еІӣз»қеҜ№дјҡи®©д»–д»¬еҝғж»Ўж„Ҹи¶ігҖӮеә“е…Ӣеұұжө·жӢ”3764зұіпјҢжҳҜе…¶жңҖй«ҳеі°пјҢиҝҳжңүеҸҰеӨ–16еә§еұұеі°жө·жӢ”и¶…иҝҮ3000зұігҖӮиҮӘз„¶иҖҢз„¶ең°пјҢи®ёеӨҡеӣҪеҶ…еӨ–зҡ„зҷ»еұұиҖ…йғҪжқҘеҲ°иҝҷйҮҢжҢ‘жҲҳеҚ—йҳҝе°”еҚ‘ж–Ҝеұұи„үгҖӮжӯӨеӨ–пјҢиҝҷзүҮй«ҳең°ең°еҢәиҝҳжңүе№ҝйҳ”зҡ„еҶ°е·қзі»з»ҹгҖҒж— еһ зҡ„жЈ®жһ—е’Ңж— ж•°зҡ„ж№–жіҠгҖӮеңЁй•ҝиҫҫ500е…¬йҮҢзҡ„еҚ—йҳҝе°”еҚ‘ж–Ҝеұұи„үдёӯпјҢиғҪйўҶз•ҘеҲ°дёҖдәӣдё–з•ҢдёҠжңҖжЈ’зҡ„еұұең°йЈҺе…үгҖӮ

жҲ–и®ёдҪ жӣҙе–ңж¬ўеӨ§жө·пјҹеҚ—еІӣдёҚд»…жҳҜдёҖдёӘеІӣеұҝпјҢе…¶жө·еІёзәҝеӨ–иҝҳжңүи®ёеӨҡе°ҸеІӣеұҝгҖӮиҝҷйҮҢжңүз»қдҪізҡ„ж·ұжө·жҚ•йұјгҖҒж°ҙиӮәжҪңж°ҙе’Ңжө®жҪңжқЎд»¶пјҢе°Ҫз®ЎиҝҷйҮҢзҡ„жө·ж°ҙжҜ”еҢ—еІӣзҡ„жө·ж°ҙжӣҙеҮүзҲҪгҖӮпјҲи®°дҪҸпјҢеңЁеҚ—еҚҠзҗғпјҢи¶ҠеҫҖеҢ—иө°пјҢж°”еҖҷи¶Ҡжё©жҡ–гҖӮпјүз”ұдәҺеҚ—еІӣзҡ„дәәеҸЈжҜ”еҢ—еІӣе°‘пјҢйӮЈдәӣжёҙжңӣзӢ¬еӨ„е’ҢзәҜеҮҖжө·ж»©зҡ„дәәдјҡеңЁиҝҷйҮҢжғҠе–ңең°еҸ‘зҺ°иҮӘе·ұиҝҗж°”зңҹеҘҪгҖӮеҚ—еІӣеҮ д№ҺжІЎжңүйҮҚе·ҘдёҡпјҢз©әж°”гҖҒж°ҙе’Ңеңҹең°йғҪжІЎжңүжұЎжҹ“гҖӮеӣ жӯӨпјҢеҪ“ең°зҡ„жө·йІңе№ІеҮҖгҖҒдё°еҜҢдё”жәҗжәҗдёҚж–ӯгҖӮ

жңүзӣҠеҒҘеә·зҡ„ж°”еҖҷеҗёеј•дҪ еҗ—пјҹеҚ—еІӣе°ұжҳҜиҝҷж ·зҡ„ең°ж–№гҖӮйҷӨдәҶй«ҳең°е’ҢеұұеҢәпјҢиҝҷйҮҢж°”еҖҷжё©е’ҢпјҢйҷҚйӣӘеҫҲе°‘гҖӮе’ҢзҲұе°”е…°гҖҒиӢұж је…°дёҖж ·пјҢиҝҷйҮҢд№ҹжІЎжңүжһҒз«Ҝзҡ„ж°”жё©гҖӮеӨҸеӨ©жё©жҡ–дҪҶдёҚзӮҺзғӯпјҢеҶ¬еӨ©еҮүзҲҪдҪҶдёҚеҜ’еҶ·гҖӮиҝҷзүҮең°еҢәе……жІӣзҡ„йҷҚйӣЁжңүж—¶дјҡи®©ж–°йІңз©әж°”еҸҳеҫ—ж№ҝж¶ҰгҖӮжҜҸдёӘеӯЈиҠӮйғҪеҗёеј•зқҖзғӯзҲұеӨ§иҮӘз„¶зҡ„дәәеҲ°жҲ·еӨ–еҺ»пјҢеңЁд№Ўжқ‘е°Ҫжғ…жҙ»еҠЁгҖӮ

еҪ“з„¶пјҢеҚ—еІӣе№¶йқһйҖӮеҗҲжүҖжңүдәәгҖӮеҜ№дәҺйӮЈдәӣйңҖиҰҒз№ҒеҝҷгҖҒжӢҘжҢӨгҖҒеҳҲжқӮе’ҢжұЎжҹ“еҹҺеёӮзҡ„дәәжқҘиҜҙпјҢиҝҷдёӘеҚ—ж–№еүҚе“ЁиӮҜе®ҡдјҡи®©д»–д»¬еӨұжңӣгҖӮеҜ№дәҺйӮЈдәӣе–ңж¬ўеҺӢеҠӣе’Ңзҙ§еј зҡ„дәәжқҘиҜҙпјҢеҚ—еІӣд№ҹдёҚдјҡз»ҷд»–д»¬еёҰжқҘж»Ўи¶іж„ҹгҖӮиҖҢеҜ№дәҺйӮЈдәӣе®Ғж„ҝеҫ…еңЁе®¶йҮҢжҲ–еҠһе…¬е®ӨеҜ№зқҖз”өи„‘еұҸе№•пјҢжҲ–иҖ…еңЁиҝӘж–Ҝз§‘иҲһеҺ…йңҮиҖіж¬ІиҒӢгҖҒзғҹйӣҫејҘжј«зҡ„иҲһжұ йҮҢзҡ„дәәжқҘиҜҙпјҢдё–з•ҢдёҠдёҖдәӣжңҖжЈ’зҡ„иҮӘз„¶йЈҺе…үж°ёиҝңж— жі•еҗёеј•д»–д»¬зҰ»ејҖгҖӮз„¶иҖҢпјҢеҜ№дәҺжҲ‘д»¬е…¶дҪҷзҡ„дәәжқҘиҜҙпјҢеҚ—еІӣжҳҜдё–з•ҢдёҠжңҖйҡҗз§ҳзҡ„з‘°е®қгҖӮеҰӮжһңеӨ§иҮӘз„¶зҡ„еӨ©е Ӯеҗ¬иө·жқҘеҫҲиҜұдәәпјҢдёҖе®ҡиҰҒеҺ»жёёи§ҲдёҖдёӢеҚ—еІӣгҖӮ

иҮӘз„¶ең°зҗҶдёӯдёҖдёӘиҫғдёәеҘҮзү№зҡ„е·§еҗҲжҳҜпјҢдё–з•ҢдёҠеӯҳеңЁзқҖдёӨз»„еӨ§е°Ҹзӣёиҝ‘зҡ„еҸҢеІӣпјҢе®ғд»¬еҲҶеҲ«дҪҚдәҺеҪјжӯӨзҡ„еҜ№жҳ зӮ№пјҢд№ҹе°ұжҳҜең°зҗғзҡ„дёӨжһҒгҖӮеҢ—еҚҠзҗғзҡ„иӢұж је…°е’ҢзҲұе°”е…°зҫӨеІӣд»ҘеҸҠеҚ—еҚҠзҗғзҡ„ж–°иҘҝе…°еҚ—еҢ—дәҢеІӣе°ұжҳҜиҝҷж ·зҡ„е·§еҗҲгҖӮеүҚдёӨиҖ…жһ„жҲҗдәҶиҒ”еҗҲзҺӢеӣҪе’ҢзҲұе°”е…°е…ұе’ҢеӣҪпјҢеҗҺдёӨиҖ…еҲҷз»„жҲҗдәҶж–°иҘҝе…°гҖӮеңЁиҝҷеӣӣдёӘеІӣеұҝдёӯпјҢеҚ—еІӣж— з–‘жҳҜжұЎжҹ“жңҖе°‘дё”йЈҺжҷҜжңҖдёәз§ҖдёҪзҡ„гҖӮ

Vocabulary & Idioms

  • odderвҖ”вҖ”еҘҮжҖӘзҡ„
  • physical geographyвҖ”вҖ”иҮӘз„¶ең°зҗҶеӯҰ
  • there are two double islandsвҖ”вҖ”жңүдёӨеҜ№еІӣеұҝ
  • antipodesвҖ”вҖ”еҜ№и·–ең°
  • hemisphereвҖ”вҖ”еҚҠзҗғ
  • be dotted withвҖ”вҖ”зӮ№зјҖзқҖ
  • ruggedвҖ”вҖ”еҙҺеІ–зҡ„
  • mysticalвҖ”вҖ”зҘһз§ҳзҡ„
  • quaintвҖ”вҖ”еҸӨйӣ…зҡ„
  • low-lyingвҖ”вҖ”дҪҺжҙјзҡ„
  • balmyвҖ”вҖ”жё©е’Ңзҡ„
  • traverseвҖ”вҖ”з©ҝиҝҮ
  • AlpsвҖ”вҖ”йҳҝе°”еҚ‘ж–Ҝеұұ
  • isletвҖ”вҖ”е°ҸеІӣ
  • scubaвҖ”вҖ”ж°ҙиӮә
  • snorkelingвҖ”вҖ”жҪңж°ҙ
  • never-endingвҖ”вҖ”ж°ёж— жӯўеўғзҡ„
  • briskвҖ”вҖ”иҪ»еҝ«зҡ„
  • outpostвҖ”вҖ”иҫ№иҝңең°еҢә
  • empty-handedз©әжүӢ
  • thumpingвҖ”вҖ”з °з °е“Қзҡ„
  • enticeвҖ”вҖ”еј•иҜұ
  • make a point of + V-ingвҖ”вҖ”еҝ…е®ҡпјҢжҖ»жҳҜиҰҒеҒҡ
    • On the first day of each month I make a point of paying my rent in person.

entry.title: й«ҳзү©з¬¬дёүз«
entry.content:
й«ҳеҲҶеӯҗжә¶ж¶І

жә¶ж¶Іпјҡжә¶иҙЁ+жә¶еүӮ

  • ж°”жҖҒжә¶ж¶ІпјҲж··еҗҲж°”дҪ“пјү
  • ж¶ІжҖҒжә¶ж¶І
  • еӣәжҖҒжә¶ж¶ІпјҲеҰӮеҗҲйҮ‘пјү

й«ҳеҲҶеӯҗжә¶ж¶Іпјҡй«ҳиҒҡзү©д»ҘеҲҶеӯҗзҠ¶жҖҒеҲҶж•ЈеңЁжә¶еүӮдёӯжүҖеҪўжҲҗзҡ„еқҮзӣёж··еҗҲзү©пјҢзғӯеҠӣеӯҰдёҠзЁіе®ҡзҡ„дәҢе…ғжҲ–еӨҡе…ғдҪ“зі»

  • жңӘзЎ«еҢ–зҡ„еӨ©з„¶ж©Ўиғ¶пјҲз”ҹиғ¶пјү+жұҪжІ№пјҢиӢҜгҖҒз”ІиӢҜ
  • HDPE+еӣӣж°ўиҗҳ
  • иҒҡд№ҷзғҜйҶҮ+ж°ҙ

й«ҳеҲҶеӯҗжә¶ж¶Ізҡ„з”ЁйҖ”

  • зЁҖжә¶ж¶ІпјҲC<1%пјүпјҡдҫӢеҰӮеҲҶеӯҗйҮҸжөӢе®ҡ
    • зғӯеҠӣеӯҰжҖ§иҙЁзҡ„з ”з©¶
    • еҠЁеҠӣеӯҰжҖ§иҙЁзҡ„з ”з©¶пјҲжә¶ж¶Ізҡ„жІүйҷҚпјҢжү©ж•ЈпјҢзІҳеәҰзӯүпјү
    • й«ҳеҲҶеӯҗеңЁжә¶ж¶Ідёӯзҡ„еҪўжҖҒе°әеҜёпјҲжҹ”йЎәжҖ§пјҢж”ҜеҢ–жғ…еҶө зӯүпјүз ”з©¶е…¶зӣёдә’дҪңз”ЁпјҲеҢ…жӢ¬й«ҳеҲҶеӯҗй“ҫж®өй—ҙпјҢй“ҫж®өдёҺжә¶еүӮеҲҶеӯҗй—ҙзҡ„зӣёдә’дҪңз”Ёпјү
    • жөӢйҮҸеҲҶеӯҗйҮҸпјҢеҲҶеӯҗйҮҸеҲҶеёғпјҢжөӢе®ҡеҶ…иҒҡиғҪеҜҶеәҰпјҢи®Ўз®— зЎ«еҢ–иғ¶зҡ„дәӨиҒ”еҜҶеәҰзӯү
  • жө“жә¶ж¶ІпјҲC>5%пјүпјҡзәәдёқгҖҒжІ№жјҶгҖҒиғ¶зІҳеүӮгҖҒеўһеЎ‘

жө“жә¶ж¶Іе’ҢзЁҖжә¶ж¶ІжңҖжң¬иҙЁзҡ„еҢәеҲ«пјҡзЁҖжә¶ж¶ІдёӯеҚ•дёӘеӨ§еҲҶеӯҗй“ҫзәҝеӣўжҳҜеӯӨз«ӢеӯҳеңЁзҡ„пјҢзӣёдә’д№Ӣй—ҙжІЎжңүдәӨеҸ пјӣиҖҢеңЁжө“еҺҡдҪ“зі»дёӯпјҢеӨ§еҲҶеӯҗй“ҫд№Ӣй—ҙеҸ‘з”ҹиҒҡйӣҶе’Ңзј з»“гҖӮ

й«ҳеҲҶеӯҗзҡ„жә¶и§Ј

жә¶и§ЈиҝҮзЁӢ

  • е°ҸеҲҶеӯҗжә¶и§Јпјҡжә¶иҙЁеҗ‘жә¶еүӮдёӯжү©ж•Ј
  • й«ҳеҲҶеӯҗжә¶и§Јпјҡ
    • зәҝеҪўж— е®ҡеҪўй«ҳеҲҶеӯҗ
      • ж··еҗҲеҲқжңҹпјҡеҚ•йЎ№жү©ж•ЈпјҢжә¶иғҖ
      • ж··еҗҲеҗҺжңҹпјҡеҸҢеҗ‘жү©ж•ЈпјҢжә¶и§Ј
    • зәҝеҪўз»“жҷ¶й«ҳеҲҶеӯҗвҖ”вҖ”е…Ҳжә¶иғҖж— е®ҡеҪўеҢәпјҢеңЁжҷ¶дҪ“зҶ”зӮ№йҷ„иҝ‘зҡ„жё©еәҰдҪҝжҷ¶дҪ“и§ЈдҪ“еҗҺжә¶и§Ј
    • дәӨиҒ”й«ҳеҲҶеӯҗвҖ”вҖ”иҫҫеҲ°жә¶иғҖе№іиЎЎеҗҺеҲҶеӯҗжү©ж•ЈеҚіе‘ҠеҒңжӯўпјҢеҸӘжә¶иғҖпјҢдёҚе®Ңе…Ёжә¶и§Ј
      • еҸҜжә¶йғЁеҲҶпјҡжә¶иғ¶
      • **дёҚеҸҜжә¶йғЁеҲҶпјҡеҮқиғ¶гҖҒеҸӘиғҪжә¶иғҖ
      • й«ҳеәҰдәӨиҒ”пјҲCйҳ¶зғӯеӣәжҖ§ж ‘и„ӮпјүпјҡдёҚжә¶иғҖ**

жә¶еүӮзҡ„йҖүжӢ©

  • жһҒжҖ§зӣёиҝ‘

  • жә¶еүӮеҢ–дҪңз”ЁвҖ”вҖ”е…·жңүзӣёејӮз”өжҖ§зҡ„дёӨз§ҚеҹәеӣўпјҢжһҒжҖ§ејәејұи¶ҠжҺҘиҝ‘пјҢеҪјжӯӨй—ҙзӣёдә’дҪңз”Ёи¶ҠејәгҖҒз»“еҗҲеҠӣи¶ҠеӨ§гҖӮ

жә¶еүӮ-й«ҳеҲҶеӯҗзӣёдә’дҪңз”Ё>й«ҳеҲҶеӯҗ-й«ҳеҲҶеӯҗй—ҙзӣёдә’дҪңз”Ё

  • еҶ…иҒҡиғҪеҜҶеәҰжҲ–жә¶еәҰеҸӮж•°зӣёиҝ‘

О”E=О”HвҲ’RT\Delta \rm{E}=\Delta \rm{H}-\rm{RT}О”E=О”HвҲ’RT

CED=О”EV0\rm{CED=\dfrac{\Delta E}{V_0}}CED=V0вҖӢО”EвҖӢ

Оҙ=CED=вҲ‘FV=ПҒвҲ‘FM0\rm{\delta=\sqrt{CED}=\dfrac{\sum F}{V}=\dfrac{\rho\sum F}{M_0}}Оҙ=CEDвҖӢ=VвҲ‘FвҖӢ=M0вҖӢПҒвҲ‘FвҖӢ

зңҹе®һзҡ„жә¶и§Јжғ…еҶөйңҖиҰҒе°Ҷдёүз§Қеӣ зҙ з»јеҗҲиҖғиҷ‘

  • PAN**пјҲиҒҡдёҷзғҜи…ҲгҖҒејәжһҒжҖ§пјүпјҡеҢ…жӢ¬еҗҲжҲҗзәӨз»ҙпјҲеҰӮи…Ҳзә¶пјҢд№ҹз§°дәәйҖ зҫҠжҜӣпјүгҖӮжә¶дәҺDMFпјҲдәҢз”Іеҹәз”Ій…°иғәпјүгҖҒд№ҷи…ҲпјҲејәжһҒжҖ§пјүпјҢдҪҶдёҚжә¶и§ЈдәҺдёҺе®ғОҙеҖјзӣёиҝ‘зҡ„д№ҷйҶҮгҖҒз”ІйҶҮзӯүгҖӮеӣ дёәPANжһҒжҖ§еҫҲејәпјҢиҖҢд№ҷйҶҮгҖҒз”ІйҶҮзӯүжә¶еүӮжһҒжҖ§еӨӘејұгҖӮ
  • ** **PSпјҲиҒҡиӢҜд№ҷзғҜгҖҒејұжһҒжҖ§пјүпјҡ**и„ҶжҖ§еЎ‘ж–ҷгҖӮжә¶дәҺз”ІиӢҜгҖҒж°Ҝд»ҝгҖҒиӢҜиғәпјҲејұжһҒжҖ§пјүе’ҢиӢҜпјҲйқһжһҒжҖ§пјүгҖӮдёҚиғҪжә¶и§ЈеңЁдёҺе®ғОҙеҖјзӣёиҝ‘зҡ„дёҷй…®дёӯпјҢеӣ дёәPSејұжһҒжҖ§пјҢиҖҢдёҷй…®ејәжһҒжҖ§гҖӮ

зғӯеҠӣеӯҰеҲҶжһҗ

О”Gm=О”HmвҲ’TО”Sm\rm{\Delta G_m=\Delta H_m -T \Delta S_m}О”GmвҖӢ=О”HmвҖӢвҲ’TО”SmвҖӢ

  • жһҒжҖ§й«ҳеҲҶеӯҗ+жһҒжҖ§жә¶ж¶І

    • жә¶и§Јж”ҫзғӯпјҢО”Gm<0\rm{\Delta G_m <0}О”GmвҖӢ<0пјҢжә¶и§ЈиҝҮзЁӢиғҪиҮӘеҸ‘иҝӣиЎҢ

  • йқһжһҒжҖ§жә¶ж¶ІвҖ”вҖ”Hildebrandе…¬ејҸ

  • О”Hm=VП•1П•2(Оҙ1вҲ’Оҙ2)2\rm{\Delta H_m=V\phi_1\phi_2(\delta_1-\delta_2)^2}О”HmвҖӢ=VП•1вҖӢП•2вҖӢ(Оҙ1вҖӢвҲ’Оҙ2вҖӢ)2

й«ҳеҲҶеӯҗжә¶ж¶Ізҡ„зғӯеҠӣеӯҰзҗҶи®ә

дәҢе…ғж··еҗҲдҪ“зі»дёӯдёӨз§ҚеҲҶеӯҗдёӯеҗ„еҗ«xAx_AxAвҖӢе’ҢxBx_BxBвҖӢдёӘеҚ•е…ғпјҢеҸҜжңүдёүз§ҚдёҚеҗҢжғ…еҶө

жә¶ж¶ІXAX_AXAвҖӢXBX_BXBвҖӢ
е°ҸеҲҶеӯҗжә¶ж¶І11
й«ҳеҲҶеӯҗжә¶ж¶І1x
й«ҳеҲҶеӯҗе…ұж··x1x_1x1вҖӢx2x_2x2вҖӢ

зҗҶжғіжә¶ж¶Ізҡ„зғӯеҠӣеӯҰжҖ§иҙЁ

О”Sm=вҲ’k[N1lnвҒЎX1+N2lnвҒЎX2]\rm{\Delta S_m=-k[N_1\ln X_1+N_2\ln X_2]}О”SmвҖӢ=вҲ’k[N1вҖӢlnX1вҖӢ+N2вҖӢlnX2вҖӢ]

О”Hm=0\rm{\Delta H_m=0}О”HmвҖӢ=0

О”Vm=0\rm{\Delta V_m=0}О”VmвҖӢ=0

О”P=P10X2\rm{\Delta P=P_1^0X_2}О”P=P10вҖӢX2вҖӢ

Flory-HugginsзҗҶи®ә

еҹәжң¬еҒҮе®ҡпјҡ

  • i.жә¶ж¶ІдҪ“зі»иҷҡжӢҹдёәдјјжҷ¶ж јз»“жһ„гҖӮдёҖдёӘжә¶еүӮеҲҶеӯҗеҚ дёҖдёӘжҷ¶ж јпјҢдёҖдёӘй«ҳеҲҶеӯҗеҲҶдёәx дёӘй“ҫж®өгҖҒеҚ x дёӘзӣёиҝһзҡ„жҷ¶ж јгҖӮ
  • ii.зӯүеҮ зҺҮгҖӮжә¶еүӮдёҺй“ҫж®өеҚ жҹҗдёӘд»»йҖүж јеӯҗзҡ„еҮ зҺҮжӯЈжҜ”дәҺе…¶еңЁдҪ“зі»дёӯзҡ„еҲҶж•°гҖӮ
  • iii.зӯүжһ„иұЎиғҪгҖӮй«ҳеҲҶеӯҗй“ҫжһ„иұЎиғҪзӣёзӯүгҖӮ

ж··еҗҲзҶө

О”Sm=вҲ’k=[N1lnвҒЎN1N1+xN2+N2lnвҒЎxN2N1+xN2]\rm{\Delta S_m=-k=[N_1\ln {\dfrac{N_1}{N_1+xN_2}}+N_2\ln{\dfrac{xN_2}{N_1+xN_2}}]}О”SmвҖӢ=вҲ’k=[N1вҖӢlnN1вҖӢ+xN2вҖӢN1вҖӢвҖӢ+N2вҖӢlnN1вҖӢ+xN2вҖӢxN2вҖӢвҖӢ]

П•1=N1N1+xN2\phi_1=\dfrac{N_1}{N_1+xN_2}П•1вҖӢ=N1вҖӢ+xN2вҖӢN1вҖӢвҖӢ

П•2=xN2N1+xN2\phi_2=\dfrac{xN_2}{N_1+xN_2}П•2вҖӢ=N1вҖӢ+xN2вҖӢxN2вҖӢвҖӢ

ж··еҗҲдҪ“зі»дёӯжә¶еүӮеҲҶеӯҗзҡ„дҪ“з§ҜеҲҶж•°дёәП•1\phi_1П•1вҖӢпјҢй«ҳеҲҶеӯҗдёәП•2\phi_2П•2вҖӢ

ж··еҗҲз„“гҖҒ ж··еҗҲиҮӘз”ұиғҪ

entry.title: дёҖз§ҚжҺәжқӮзЎ«й“¶й”—зҹҝз”өи§ЈиҙЁзҡ„еҗҲжҲҗ
entry.content:
19ж—Ҙжҷҡз¬¬дёҖж¬Ўиҝӣе®һйӘҢе®ӨпјҢеёҲе§җеёҰжҲ‘еҗҲжҲҗдёҖз§ҚеӣәжҖҒз”өи§ЈиҙЁпјҢеҸӮиҖғж–ҮзҢ®жңүAngew. Chem. Int. Ed. 2019, 58, 8681 вҖ“8686зӯүгҖӮз”ұдәҺз”өжұ жҳҜй”Ӯз”өжұ пјҢйңҖиҰҒйҡ”з»қH2O\rm{H_2O}H2вҖӢOе’ҢO2\rm{O_2}O2вҖӢпјҢжүҖжңүз”өи§ЈиҙЁеҺҹж–ҷзҡ„з§°йҮҸж“ҚдҪңйңҖиҰҒеңЁжүӢеҘ—з®ұдёӯиҝӣиЎҢпјҢжүӢеҘ—з®ұдёӯзҡ„з§°йҮҸз®—жҳҜеҜ№ж–°дәәзҡ„з¬¬дёҖеӨ§жҢ‘жҲҳеҗ§е“Ҳе“Ҳ~

жүӢеҘ—з®ұд№ӢеүҚд№ҹз”ЁиҝҮпјҢд»ҠжҷҡйЎәдҫҝеӨҚд№ дәҶдёҖдёӢ

зЁҚеҫ®жҖ»з»“дәҶдёҖдёӢжӯҘйӘӨпјҡ

  • зҙ§й—ӯеҶ…иҲұй—ЁпјҢжү“ејҖ иҝҮжёЎиҲұеӨ–иҲұй—Ё
  • ж”ҫе…Ҙиҙ§зү©е№¶е…ій—ӯеӨ–иҲұй—Ё
  • иҝӣиЎҢдёүж¬Ўе……ж”ҫж°”ж“ҚдҪңпјҲе…ҲжҠҪеҗҺе……пјүпјҢз„¶еҗҺеҒңжӯўе……ж”ҫж°”
  • жү“ејҖеҶ…иҲұй—ЁеҸ–еҮәиҙ§зү©

жүӢеҘ—з®ұеҶ…зҡ„зҘһеҷЁвҖ”вҖ”й•Ҡеӯҗ

жҲ‘еңЁжүӢеҘ—з®ұеҶ…дё»иҰҒжҳҜз§°йҮҸпјҢеҰӮдёӢиҜ•еүӮпјҡ

иҜ•еүӮжҖ§зҠ¶еӨҮжіЁиҙЁйҮҸ
Li2S\rm{Li_2S}Li2вҖӢSзҷҪиүІжҷ¶дҪ“пјҢеҸҚиҗӨзҹіз»“жһ„жңүзӮ№еғҸзҷҪзі–пјҲ0.3446g(0.0074995 mol)
P2S5\rm{P_2S_5}P2вҖӢS5вҖӢй»„иүІзүҮеұӮзҠ¶жҷ¶дҪ“жҳ“ж°ҙи§Ј0.4168g(0.001875 mol)
LiCl\rm{LiCl}LiClзҷҪиүІжҷ¶дҪ“пјҢжҳ“жҪ®и§ЈжңүзӮ№зІҳпјҢйҡҫз§°0.2384g(0.005624 mol)

Li:P:S:Cl=0.020623:0.00375:0.0168745:0.005624=вүҲ5.5:1:4.5:1.5\rm{Li:P:S:Cl}=0.020623:0.00375:0.0168745:0.005624=\approx5.5:1:4.5:1.5Li:P:S:Cl=0.020623:0.00375:0.0168745:0.005624=вүҲ5.5:1:4.5:1.5

x=0.5(жң¬ж¬Ўе®һйӘҢ)

е…ЁеӣәжҖҒй”Ӯз”өжұ зҡ„еҸ‘еұ•дҫқиө–дәҺй«ҳжҖ§иғҪеӣәжҖҒз”өи§ЈиҙЁпјҢйңҖе…·еӨҮй«ҳзҰ»еӯҗз”өеҜјзҺҮе’ҢиүҜеҘҪе»¶еұ•жҖ§зӯүзү№жҖ§гҖӮй”ӮзЎ«й“¶й”—зҹҝ(Li6PS5X\rm{Li_6PS_5X}Li6вҖӢPS5вҖӢX)иҷҪжңүжҪңеҠӣпјҢдҪҶжӯӨеүҚе®Өжё©зҰ»еӯҗз”өеҜјзҺҮзӯүжҖ§иғҪжңүеҫ…жҸҗеҚҮпјҢжң¬ж¬Ўе®һйӘҢе°қиҜ•еӨҚзҺ°ж–ҮзҢ®жҸҗй«ҳз”өи§ЈиҙЁзҡ„зҰ»еӯҗз”өеҜјзҺҮгҖӮ

  • Li6вҲ’xPS5вҲ’xCl1+x\rm{Li_{6 - x}PS_{5 - x}Cl_{1 + x}}Li6вҲ’xвҖӢPS5вҲ’xвҖӢCl1+xвҖӢеӣәжә¶дҪ“зӣёпјҢеҸ‘зҺ°еўһеҠ ClвҲ’S2вҲ’\rm{\dfrac{Cl^-}{S^{2-}}}S2вҲ’ClвҲ’вҖӢжҜ”дҫӢеҸҜзі»з»ҹдё”жҳҫи‘—ең°еҪұе“Қжҷ¶ж јдёӯй”ӮзҰ»еӯҗжү©ж•ЈжҖ§пјҢжҸҗеҚҮз”өеҜјзҺҮгҖӮеҰӮ==Li5.5PS4.5Cl1.5\rm{Li_{5.5}PS_{4.5}Cl_{1.5}}Li5.5вҖӢPS4.5вҖӢCl1.5вҖӢеңЁ 298K ж—¶еҶ·еҺӢз”өеҜјзҺҮиҫҫ9.4Вұ0.1mS/cm\rm{9.4\pm 0.1 mS/cm}9.4Вұ0.1mS/cmпјҢзғ§з»“еҗҺдёә12.0Вұ0.2mS/cm\rm{12.0\pm 0.2 mS/cm}12.0Вұ0.2mS/cmпјҢеҮ д№ҺжҳҜзӣёеҗҢжқЎд»¶дёӢLi6PS5Cl\rm{Li_{6}PS_{5}Cl}Li6вҖӢPS5вҖӢClзҡ„еӣӣеҖҚ==
  • еҸ‘зҺ°еҚӨеҢ–зү©еҸ–д»ЈйҷҚдҪҺдәҶй”ӮзҰ»еӯҗдёҺе‘ЁеӣҙжЎҶжһ¶йҳҙзҰ»еӯҗзҡ„зӣёдә’дҪңз”ЁпјҢеўһеҠ дәҶдҪҚзӮ№ж— еәҸеәҰе’Ңй”Ӯз©әдҪҚж•°йҮҸпјҢиҝӣиҖҢжҸҗеҚҮй”ӮзҰ»еӯҗжү©ж•ЈжҖ§пјҢжҳҺзЎ®дәҶзҰ»еӯҗдј иҫ“жңәеҲ¶

еңЁжүӢеҘ—з®ұеҶ…е°Ҷдёүз§ҚиҜ•еүӮз§°йҮҸеҗҺж”ҫе…Ҙз ”зЈЁзҪҗеҶ…пјҢе’ҢзҰ»еҝғжңәзұ»дјјпјҢзҗғзЈЁжңәзҡ„з ”зЈЁзҪҗд№ҹйңҖиҰҒй…Қе№іпјҲжңҖеҘҪе…Ҳз”ЁиҪ¬еӯҗзҗғй…Қе№іеҶҚз§°йҮҸиҚҜе“ҒпјүпјҢй…Қе№іеҗҺж”ҫе…ҘжңәеҷЁеҶ…и®ҫзҪ®еҸӮж•°

е·ҘдҪңеҺҹзҗҶпјҡеҪ“д»ӘеҷЁеҗҜеҠЁж—¶пјҢз”өжңәеёҰеҠЁе…¬е…ұзҡ„еӨӘйҳіиҪ®иҪ¬еҠЁпјҢиҖҢдҪҚдәҺеӨӘйҳіиҪ®дёҠзҡ„з ”зЈЁзҪҗеҲҷеӣҙз»•иҮӘиә«зҡ„иҪҙдҪңиҮӘиҪ¬иҝҗеҠЁпјҢз ”зЈЁзҪҗиҮӘиҪ¬зҡ„ж–№еҗ‘дёҺеӨӘйҳіиҪ®зҡ„ж–№еҗ‘зӣёеҸҚгҖӮз ”зЈЁзҗғдёҺз ”зЈЁзҪҗдёҖиө·иҝҗиҪ¬ж—¶пјҢеҸ—еҲ°иҮӘиҪ¬еҒҸеҗ‘еҠӣзҡ„еҸ еҠ еҪұе“ҚпјҢеңЁиҝҷз§ҚеҪұе“ҚдёӢпјҢз ”зЈЁзҗғйҮҠж”ҫеҮәеӨ§йҮҸзҡ„еҠЁиғҪпјҢж ·е“ҒдёҚж–ӯеҸ—еҲ°з ”зЈЁзҗғзҡ„ж’һеҮ»пјҢеҗҢж—¶дёҺз ”зЈЁзҪҗеҶ…еЈҒдә§з”ҹеӨ§йҮҸж‘©ж“ҰпјҢд»ҺиҖҢиў«й«ҳеәҰзІүзўҺгҖӮ

д»ҺдёҠеҲ°дёӢзҗғзЈЁжңәзҡ„еҸӮж•°и®ҫзҪ®еҲҶеҲ«дёәпјҡ

  • иҪ¬йҖҹ
  • з ”зЈЁж—¶й—ҙ
  • дј‘жҒҜеҶ·еҚҙж—¶й—ҙ
  • еҫӘзҺҜж¬Ўж•°

жң¬ж¬Ўзҡ„з ”зЈЁж—¶й—ҙи®ҫзҪ®дёә10minпјҢдј‘жҒҜж—¶й—ҙи®ҫзҪ®дёә5minпјҢеҫӘзҺҜж¬Ўж•°дёә60пјҢйӮЈд№ҲжҖ»ж—¶й—ҙе°ұжҳҜ15h

(жңәжў°з ”зЈЁеј•еҸ‘еӣәзӣёеҸҚеә”)

20ж—ҘдёҠеҚҲ11зӮ№пјҢе°Ҷз ”зЈЁзҪҗд»ҺзҗғзЈЁжңәдёҠеҚёдёӢжқҘпјҲе’Ңж°ҙзғӯеҗҲжҲҗеҸҚеә”йҮңжңүзӮ№еғҸе“Ҳе“Ҳ~пјүпјҢеҝҳдәҶеҺӢдҪҸз ”зЈЁзҪҗзӣ–еӯҗпјҢеӨ§еҠӣеҮәеҘҮиҝ№жҠҠзӣ–еӯҗжӢ”еҮәжқҘдәҶпјҢз”өи§ЈиҙЁжҺҘи§ҰеҲ°з©әж°”дәҶрҹҳҮпјҢе°ұеҪ“жҲҗеҜ№з…§з»„еҗ§пјҲжң¬ж¬ЎеҗҲжҲҗдәҶдёӨз»„зӣёеҗҢзҡ„пјҢе§‘дё”и®Өдёәзӣ–дёҠзӣ–еӯҗе°ұжҳҜдёҺеӨ–з•Ңж°”дҪ“зӣёйҡ”з»қе“Ҳе“ҲпјүпјҢ

е°ҶдёӨдёӘзҪҗеӯҗиҪ¬з§»еҲ°жүӢеҘ—з®ұеҶ…пјҢйҰ–е…ҲиҪ¬з§»еҲ°з§°йҮҸзәёдёҠпјҢз„¶еҗҺиҪ¬з§»еҲ°е°ҸзҰ»еҝғз®ЎеҶ…пјҢиҝҷдёҖжӯҘе·Ёйҡҫж“ҚдҪңпјҢз ”зЈЁеҗҺзҡ„з”өи§ЈиҙЁжңүдәӣз»“еқ—дәҶпјҢйңҖиҰҒжүЈдёӢжқҘпјҢжңҖдё»иҰҒжҳҜз ”зЈЁзҪҗжҜ”иҫғйҮҚпјҢжҲ‘з»ҷдёҚе°Ҹеҝғеј„ж’’дәҶдёҖзӮ№рҹҳ‘пјҢиҪ¬з§»еҲ°дёӨдёӘе°ҸзҰ»еҝғз®ЎеҗҺжҠҠдёӨдёӘз ”зЈЁзҪҗд»ҺжүӢеҘ—з®ұеҶ…жӢҝеҮәжқҘпјҢејҖе§Ӣжҙ—еҲ·еҲ·пјҢзЎ«еҢ–зү©з”Ёж°ҙеҶІеҮ йҒҚгҖҒд№ҷйҶҮеҶІеҮ йҒҚе°ұжә¶и§ЈжҺүгҖҒжҙ—е№ІеҮҖдәҶпјҢжңҖеҗҺж”ҫе…Ҙ80ж‘„ж°ҸеәҰзғҳз®ұе°ұеҗғйҘӯеҺ»е’Ҝ~пјҲзғҳе№ІеҚіеҸҜпјҢдёҚеҸҜд№…зҪ®пјҢеҗҰеҲҷз ”зЈЁзҪҗиғ¶еңҲдјҡиҖҒеҢ–пјҢеҜҶе°ҒжҖ§е°ұдёҚеҘҪдәҶпјүдёӢеҚҲиҝҳжңүд»ӘеҷЁеҲҶжһҗе®һйӘҢиҜҫе‘ў

20ж—ҘжҷҡдёҠ19зӮ№пјҢејҖе§Ӣйҡ”з»қж°§ж°”зғ§з»“ж ·е“ҒгҖӮеңЁжүӢеҘ—з®ұеҶ…е°ҶзҰ»еҝғз®Ўдёӯзҡ„иҜ•еүӮз”Ёй•ҝйўҲжјҸж–—еҲҶеҲ«иҪ¬з§»еҲ°дёӨдёӘй•ҝзҺ»з’ғз®ЎйҮҢпјҢз„¶еҗҺжҖјдёҠйҳҖй—ЁпјҢз”Ёе°ҒеҸЈиғ¶еҜҶе°ҒдёҠпјҢз„¶еҗҺеҺ»дёҖжҘјйҰ–е…Ҳе°Ҷж°”йҳҖй—ЁжҺҘе…ҘеҺӢзј©жңәпјҢе°Ҷй•ҝзҺ»з’ғз®ЎеҶ…зҡ„ж°”дҪ“е°ҪеҸҜиғҪжҠҪеҮәпјҢжҺҘзқҖз”ЁCH4+O2\rm{CH_4+O_2}CH4вҖӢ+O2вҖӢзҡ„ж··еҗҲж°”дҪ“зҒјзғ§й•ҝзҺ»з’ғз®ЎдҪҝе…¶еҜҶе°ҒпјҲе’ҢеӨ§дёҖж—¶ж— жңәеҢ–еӯҰзҡ„дёҖж¬Ўе®һйӘҢжңүзӮ№еғҸпјҢиҮӘе·ұзғ§иғ¶еӨҙж»ҙз®Ўе“Ҳе“Ҳе“ҲрҹҳіпјүпјҢзғ§е®ҢеҗҺе°ұеҸҳжҲҗдәҶдёӨдёӘе®Ңе…ЁеҜҶе°Ғзҡ„зҺ»з’ғз®Ўе•ҰпјҢз„¶еҗҺжҠҠе®ғд»¬ж”ҫе…Ҙз”өзӮүзғ§з»“еҚіеҸҜпјҢ

entry.title: зғ§зӮүеӯҗ~
entry.content:
жң¬иҙҙдёәи®°еҪ•иҙҙпјҢеёҲе…„еёҲе§җж•ҷдәҶжҲ‘3+ж¬ЎеҰӮдҪ•и®ҫзҪ®зӮүеӯҗзҡ„зЁӢеәҸпјҢз„¶й№…жҲ‘иҝҳжҳҜжҳ“еҝҳдәҺжҳҜе°ұжңүдәҶжң¬её–рҹҳў

зЁӢеәҸдјјд№ҺйғҪжҳҜйҖҡз”Ёзҡ„еҗ§пјҢй©¬еј—зӮүгҖҒз®ЎејҸзӮүгҖҒз”өзӮүвҖҰвҖҰе•Ҙж—¶еҖҷйҒҮеҲ°дёҚдёҖж ·зҡ„дәҶеҶҚжӣҙж–°жң¬иҙҙеҗ§

  • S1и®ҫзҪ®еҪ“еүҚжё©еәҰ
  • S2и°ғиҠӮжңҖй«ҳжё©еәҰ
  • S3и®ҫзҪ®ж•°еҖјпјҢеҒҡе·®йҷӨд»ҘеҚҮжё©йҖҹзҺҮ
  • S4и®ҫзҪ®еҚҮжё©еҗҺзҡ„дҝқжё©жё©еәҰпјҲе’ҢжңҖеӨ§жё©еәҰж•°еҖјдёҖж ·зҡ„пјү
  • S5и®ҫзҪ®дҝқжё©ж—¶й—ҙпјҲеҚ•дҪҚminпјү
  • S6й»ҳи®Ө-121иҮӘеҠЁйҷҚжё©
  • S7ејҖеҗҜзЁӢеәҸ

entry.title: Who's Your Mommy?
entry.content:
WhoвҖҷs Your Mommy?

In the spring of 2022, I was 36 years old and jumping up and down in my bathroom, trying to figure out my future. I had ordered a fertility test online that said it would provide fast results with just a few drops of blood. The videos on the companyвҖҷs website featured a smiling blond woman jumpingвҖ”to stimulate blood flow, naturallyвҖ”and then eВӯffortlessly dribbling blood from her nger tips all over a little strip of test paper. All I had to do was be like her. Joyful. Sanguineous . Fertile.

For years, my husband, Rich, and I had gingerly walked the prime meridian between wanting and not wanting kids, usually leaning toward the вҖңnoвҖқ side. Having a baby had seemed un aВӯ ordable and impossible. On days when I nished work at 8 p.m., the thought of procreating made me laugh, then shudder .

Recently, though, IвҖҷd begun to reconsider. I was in the midst of an admittedly strange-sounding project: I was spending a year trying to change my personality. According to a scientific personality test IвҖҷd taken, I scored sky-high on neuroticism , a trait associated with anxiety and depression, and low on agreeable ness and extroversion . I lived in a constant, clenched state of dread, and it was poisoning my life. My therapist had stopped laughing at my jokes.

But I had read some scientific research suggesting that you can change your personality by behaving like the kind of person you wish you were. Several studies show that people who want to be, say, less isolated or less anxious can make a habit of socializing, meditating, or journaling. Eventually these habits will come naturally, knitting together to form new traits.

I knew that becoming a parent had the potential to change me in even more profound ways. But I had no idea how. My own mother once said to me, вҖңI canвҖҷt picture you as a mother.вҖқ The truth was, neither could I.

I wasnвҖҷt sure I could get pregnant, even if I wanted to. My age put me in a category that was, in a less delicate time, called вҖң geriatric вҖқ for pregnancy, and one doctor told me my eggs were probably of вҖңpoor quality.вҖқ The fertility test IвҖҷd ordered was meant to determine if those eggs were serviceable . In the bathroom, I unwrapped the glossy white box. The instructions said the test would take 20 minutes and require a pack of lancets . I grabbed one and stabbed it into my geriatric forefinger. Two hours, five lancets, and a graveyard of gauze and alcohol wipes later, I still hadnвҖҷt squeezed a single droplet out of my finger. Was I not jumping high enough? Was I already failing as a mother?

I was worried I wouldnвҖҷt be able to have a baby. I was also scared to death of having one.

Arguably, many things are wrong with me. I was raised by Russian immigrants who constantly worried that the вҖң dark day вҖқ was upon us, so hopeful thoughts about the future of humanity donвҖҷt come naturally. IвҖҷm not a person who is aВӯ ected by cuteness. IвҖҷve never liked holdingвҖ”or even really looking atвҖ”other peopleвҖҷs babies. I donвҖҷt like animals. I couldnвҖҷt imagine cooing and smiling at a baby as much as science says youвҖҷre supposed to for their brain development.

My neuroticism made it especially hard to decide if I wanted kids, because no process is more rife with uncertainty than parenting, and nothing scares anxious people more than uncertainty. I worried that Rich and I would ght more, and that our relationship would suВӯ er. I worried about sleep deprivation . I felt torn between my lifelong conviction that people shouldnвҖҷt create problems for themselves and my (apparent) desire to do just that.

I would wake up in the middle of the night and Google things like percent miscarriage pregnant while 36?; anxiety pregnancy miscarriage causes; Diet Coke fetal defects; pregnancy brain stops working hands stop working. These searches surfaced horrific anecdotes , but never any conclusive answers about what I should do. One time, I Googled reasons to have kids and found an article that labeled all the reasons I had come up withвҖ”like being cared for in old age and having someone who loves meвҖ”with the heading вҖңNot-So-Good Reasons to Have Children.вҖқ

But then I would remember the times we visited RichвҖҷs mom, who had dementia , in her nursing home. Her face lit up at the sight of him . вҖңMy son, my son, my only son,вҖқ sheвҖҷd say, grabbing his arm. He was the only person she still recognized. The visits were a reminder that the people who matter most at the end are your children. The readers of your blog posts wonвҖҷt make the trip.

Heather Rackin, a sociologist at Louisiana State University, found in a study that the death of a mother or sibling increased the likelihood that a woman would give birth within two years. The e proximity of death is, perhaps, a wake-up call . Who will remember us? The study was based on RackinвҖҷs personal experience: When her father died in 2017, she decided not to wait any longer to have kids. His death got her thinking, she told me, about what was important in life: the experience of being loved and the chance to provide that love for someone else. Her rst child was born in 2019.

There are many reasons to postpone or avoid having childrenвҖ” the cost, the responsibility, the existence of and use case for the NoseFrida . But in addition to the practical challenges, a narrative has taken hold: Everything changes when you become a mother.

Once they reach their 30s, many people have carefully cultivated friend groups and sourdough starters and five-year plans. They вҖңreally have a good sense of who they are, and then having a baby totally disrupts everything that they thought they knew about themselves,вҖқ says Lauren RatliВ  , a perinatal therapist in Illinois. Of course, this is where I diВ  er from the rest of my cohort . By the time I was ready to have a baby, IвҖҷd already been trying to disrupt everything about myself.

For my personality-change project, I had experimented with science- backed strategies to turn down my neuroticism and amp up my extroversion and agreeableness . I had spent hundreds of hours trying out different iterations of mindfulness , culminating in a day-long meditation retreat that almost killed me with boredom but somehow alleviated my depression. Among other agreeableness-boosting activities, I traveled to London for a вҖңconversation workshop,вҖқ where I learned techniques that can make even British people show an emotion. And to become more extroverted, I went out as much as humanly possible. I played table tennis. I did improv , and survived.

For the most part, my efforts worked: I no longer thought of talking with people as a waste of time. I became less afraid of uncertainty and disappointment. I made one very good new friend. I drank less.

I had been changing, but it was a type of change that I directly determined. I could go to happy hour, or not. I could meditate, or stop. I was aware that parenthood would transform me further , but what I found unsettling was that I couldnвҖҷt know exactly how. Bizarrely, for the biggest disruption of your life, study after study shows thereвҖҷs no вҖңtypicalвҖқ way that becoming a parent changes your personality. Some studies have found tiny average decreases in extroversion or openness among new parentsвҖ” but even those findings arenвҖҷt consistent.

Despite my progress, I was still too neurotic to feel comfortable surrendering control and letting biology mold me into someone I couldnвҖҷt predict and might not recognize.

After doctors pronounced me insufficiently fertile, Rich and I decided to just stop being careful one month and see what happened. We figured we would at least have some fun before we embarked on our arduous вҖңfertility journey.вҖқ

A short time later, on a choppy boat tour in Europe, I couldnвҖҷt stop leaning over the edge of the catamaran and hurling .

вҖңDo you think you might be pregnant?вҖқ Rich whispered as the boat crew force-fed me pita bread .

вҖңDonвҖҷt be insane ,вҖқ I said. Everyone knows that 37-year-oldsвҖ” especially infertile onesвҖ”donвҖҷt get pregnant on their first try.

A week after that, I found out that I had indeed gotten pregnant on my first try.

Being pregnant means having your brain replaced with an anxiety T-shirt cannon. I didnвҖҷt feel glowy or goddessy ; I felt crazy. None of my friends has kids, and many of them reacted to my news like IвҖҷd gotten a face tattoo . One sent me a TikTok of everything that can supposedly go wrong in pregnancy, including the possibility that vomit will come out of your eyes. (It wonвҖҷt.) I spent more and more time by myself, obsessing over which swaddles were best. (We didnвҖҷt end up using any.)

Thanks to a king tide of hormones , irritability spikes during the first and last trimesters of pregnancy. People say your baby will remember the sounds they hear in the womb , but I fear mine detected little in there other than me screaming at his father. Every few weeks, something would set me off , at a deafening volume. If theyвҖҷd overheard me, those couples therapists who say contempt is the most glaring sign of a failed relationship would probably have advised us to start divvying up our furniture.

Sometimes when I was yelling, being so mean felt amazingвҖ” as though IвҖҷd finally engulfed Rich in my distress. Obviously you need a travel stroller and a regular stroller! I always apologized, and Rich always accepted my apology. But one time he said, вҖңYou know that with a kid, thatвҖҷs not really something you can take back, right? вҖқ Sometimes, late at night, after yet another argument, I would rotate my spheroid belly toward Rich and ask, вҖңWhat if I turn out to be a bad mother?вҖқ

The rest of the pregnancy was horrible. I didnвҖҷt think it was possible to feel so tired and still be technically alive. At my baby shower, when some friends asked me how I was feeling, I quoted the Russian dissident Boris Nadezhdin responding to a question about whether he feared imprisonment or death: вҖңThe tastiest and the sweetest years of my life are already in the past.вҖқ (This is is the closest Russians get to excited.)

Three weeks before my due date, after a routine ultrasound, my high-risk ob-gyn walked briskly into the room. She looked around for something to sit on and, finding nothing, plopped down on top of a closed trash can. She told me that something was wrong with my placenta , and that the baby was in danger. And that I should now walk over to the delivery wing of the hospita l.

In the antechamber of the operating room , I hyperventilated in my paper gown and tapped out emails to all my sources and bosses : IвҖҷm having an emergency C-section today, so I wonвҖҷt be available for the next few months. My last day of caring whether people were mad at me.

Afterward, while the medical residents were rearranging my innards , I thought I heard one of them ask me something.

вҖңIвҖҷm sorry, I donвҖҷt know whatвҖҷs going on down there at all,вҖқ I said across the blue curtain.

вҖңThatвҖҷs вҖҰ probably for the best,вҖқ the resident said.

HE CAME OUT with white hair, a perfectly round face, and a grumpy expression, like the leader of a former Yugoslav republic. I called him вҖңSlobodanвҖқ a couple of times, until Rich told me to stop.

Because he was early, we panic-picked a name from our shortlistвҖ” Evan. The same day he was born, doctors whisked him away to the NICU ; I saw him only a few times before we were all sent home days later. My discharge paperwork said, вҖңMom is breastfeeding four or five times a day,вҖқ which was funny because at that point I had not done it successfully even once. It was also funny because IвҖ”quite possibly the least qualified person for the jobвҖ”was apparently вҖңMom.вҖқ

Once home, we entered the period we now refer to as вҖңCute Abu Ghraib.вҖқ Sleep deprivation addled me to the point that, on a call with the pediatrician , I forgot the babyвҖҷs name. When Evan was two weeks old, I bit into a piece of chicken and tasted something bloody and sharp. I had ground my teeth so hard during his NICU stay that IвҖҷd loosened a crown .

We agonized over whether the gyrations of the SNOO Smart Sleeper Bassinet would rattle his brain too much, then grew too exhausted to care. I became the CEO of Baby Inc., and Rich was employee No. 1; we communicated only about ointments and ounces . I finally had the big boobs of my dreams, but the only man who saw them was two feet tall and couldnвҖҷt read.

But then something interrupted the misery. One night, I was holding Evan while he was sleeping. I had read that singing to your baby was beneficial, so I decided to serenade him with one of the few songs I know by heart: вҖңForever and Ever, Amen,вҖқ by Randy Travis. Except I couldnвҖҷt seem to get through the fourth line: вҖңThis is love that I feel for you always will be.вҖқ I, a bad bitch who has never cried at a wedding, kept choking up.

Rich asked me if I was okay.

вҖңWhatever!вҖқ I said, tears rolling down my cheeks . вҖңShut up!вҖқ

I thought motherhood would be a forced march through inert babyhood and feral toddler years before we finally reached the golden time of my imagination: having a talking, precocious elementary schooler. But there I was, flooded with adoration for someone who barely registered my presence. IвҖҷd hated being pregnant, so I thought I would hate having a baby, too. But I loved him. I loved this.

Recall the research showing thereвҖҷs no one way that parenthood tends to change peopleвҖҷs personalities. Anecdotally, researchers told me that they do notice certain patterns among new parents. Most moms worry about their kid, more or less constantly, from the minute they find out theyвҖҷre pregnant. вҖң Signing up to be a parent is signing up to have a lifetime of some degree of depression and anxiety ,вҖқ Ratliff , the therapist, told me.

New parentsвҖҷ satisfaction with their romantic relationship goes down, especially for mothers, and especially in the first year. вҖң Guilt is another universal ,вҖқ says AurГ©lie Athan, a clinical psychologist at Columbia UniversityвҖҷs Teachers College, who researches the transition known as вҖң matrescence .вҖқ The creeping sense that you should be with your kid while youвҖҷre working and working while youвҖҷre with your kid apparently never goes away.

She told me that mothers become more attuned and prosocial вҖ” more caring and empathetic toward others. Athan said this is why so many mothers cry when their babies cry and have a hard time watching gory movies. вҖңMoms get a really bad taste in their mouth with violent television or looking at images of war,вҖқ she said.

ThatвҖҷs where she lost me. My son had colic ; for the first four months, he screamed like the possessed unless he was within the jiggly confines of his SNOO . The doula we hired referred to him, alternately, as вҖңMr. Cheeks,вҖқ вҖңMr. Crab,вҖқ and, sarcastically , вҖңMr. Wonderful.вҖқ If I had cried every time he cried, I wouldnвҖҷt have had time to do anything else.

Eventually, Rich and I grew desensitized, or felt like we had to match his chaotic energy with equally intense stimuli . One night, after Evan wailed in our ears for two hours, we shuffed downstairs and collapsed onto the couch. There was only one thing we could think to watch that would serve as a comedown from what had just happened: Saving Private Ryan.

вҖңDid you remember to sterilize the pump parts?вҖқ I asked Rich as the entrails of American soldiers spilled out over the beaches of Normandy.

вҖңThe sterilizer thing broke, so I had to reset it,вҖқ he said as a man stumbled around with his arm blown off .

Even within these supposedly universal rules of parenthood, that is, thereвҖҷs a lot of variability. ThatвҖҷs because life events like parenthood seem to change everyone di erently, and how youвҖҷll change is, in part, up to you. For a recent study, Ted Schwaba, a psychologist at Michigan State University, and his co-authors asked thousands of Dutch people about a life event in the past 10 years, such as a divorce or a new job, that they felt had changed who they were as a person. About 7 percent of the participants identified parenthood as the event that changed them, and on average, they felt that it had made them slightly more agreeable and conscientious .

But the big takeaway for Schwaba, from looking at all the data for all the different types of life events, was that there really was no pattern. Some people became more extroverted when they got a new job. Some became less so. Some people actually became less neuroticвҖ” that is, less depressed and anxiousвҖ” after, say, a cancer diagnosis.

To Schwaba, this research suggests that itвҖҷs how you experience an event such as parenthood, more than the event itself, that determines how youвҖҷll change. вҖңThe same event, like getting divorced, might be someoneвҖҷs worst thing thatвҖҷs ever happened to them, and for someone else, it might be the best thing thatвҖҷs ever happened to them,вҖқ he told me.

Or your personality might change not immediately after an event like childbirth, but through a long process that the event sets in motion. ItвҖҷs not the cry you hear in the delivery room that changes you; itвҖҷs the many years of researching child care and soothing boo-boos that gradually turn you into someone new. To change, you have to take steps every day to do so. Having a baby wonвҖҷt make you a better person. Behaving like a better person for your baby will.

Of all the things I wanted motherhood to change about me, neuroticism was high on the list. Before I had Evan, I felt like I was personally responsible for making life unfold perfectly , and whenever I вҖңfailedвҖқ to do so, I had a meltdown . One day a few years ago, I got a bad haircut, got stuck in traffic , and had professional photos taken that looked terrible. My response to thisвҖ”what my new-parent eyes now see as an 8-out-of-10 dayвҖ”was to chug half a bottle of wine and scream to my husband through sobs, вҖңI hate everyone and everything!вҖқ

But now so much goes wrong every single day that thereвҖҷs no time to get upset about any one thing. I recently took a flight with Evan by myself, an exercise that really underscores the first Noble Truth of Buddhism (life is suВӯ ering). As I hauled the car seat, the stroller, the baby, the diaper bag, and the trendy , impractical tote from my childless years to the TSA line, an airline attendant took one look at me and said, вҖңI know; it is too much.вҖқ

In the middle of the flight, I noticed that the two bottles of formula Evan nervously drank during takeoВӯ had caught up with him, and that he was now soaked with pee . I grabbed him under the armpits and scooted across the seats to change him in the airplaneвҖҷs postage-stamp-size bathroom . With one hand, I held him, crying, on the changing table , and with the other, I dug a clean onesie out of the bottom of the diaper bag. I fastened a million tiny onesie buttons. Then I saw that I had misaligned them and fastened them again. Next it was my turn. I couldnвҖҷt leave him on the changing table, or put him on the disgusting floor. I yanked my leggings down and held him at armвҖҷs length as I peed .

By the end of that ordeal , I felt accomplished and capable. I didnвҖҷt feel like sobbing; I felt like high-fiving myself . IвҖҷve let go in other ways, too. I show up at important meetings without makeup on. I say weird stuВӯff to strangers and donвҖҷt analyze it obsessively later. Evan has forced me to step outside myself, to break from the relentless self-focus that has contributed to both my success and my unhappiness.

My remaining neuroses are laser-directed on his well-being. I had initially planned not to breastfeed, but once I started, I got so into it that when a doctor suggested that Evan would spit up less if I cut food allergens from my diet, I stopped eating virtually anything but oats and spinach for months . When I was pregnant, weвҖҷd signed the unborn Evan up for day care , but as the end of my maternity leave loomed , I embarked on a frantic search for a nanny so he could stay close to me while I worked from home. I had always mentally mocked parents who checked to be sure their babies were still breathing at night, then found myself standing in front of his crib at 3 a.m., feeling for puffs of air from two tiny nostrils .

I yell at Rich less than I used to, because not only is he employee No. 1 of Baby Inc., but heвҖҷs the only employee, and frankly there are no other applicants for the job. In fact, the whole experience has made me kinder and more tender, like the Grinch, postвҖ“heart enlargement. IвҖҷm less worried about wasting time, because all time with a baby is essentially wastedвҖ”the most important nothing youвҖҷll ever do in your life. I even love EvanвҖҷs wet, violent вҖңkisses,вҖқ which leave his baby-teeth imprints on our jaws. When my friend Anton visited recently, he watched me make horsey noises for Evan for what probably felt like hours. вҖңI canвҖҷt believe you love an infant!вҖқ he said.

During my interview with RatliВӯ , I told her that Evan had lately been losing interest in breastfeeding. I had awaited this day through months of bleeding nipples and frustration , but now that it was here, it was making me a bit sad. вҖңYour babyвҖҷs moving to the next stage,вҖқ she alarmed, вҖңand this one is not going to come back again.вҖқ I started tearing up вҖ”both at the memory of those bleary, milk-soaked months together and at the realization that he wouldnвҖҷt even be a baby for much longer.

During my personality-change experiment, my meditation teacher had tried to hammer home the idea that вҖң this too shall pass вҖқ is both uplifting and sad: Nothing bad lasts forever, but neither does anything good. Before I had Evan, I was focused on impermanence вҖҷs upsides: This uncomfortable improv show will end; this awful pregnancy will too. But now IвҖҷm more keenly aware of its downsides. The sleepless nights will end, but so too will the times Evan squeals at a game of peekaboo, or spends an entire swim class gazing up at me in awe. Every day brings a sigh of relief and a pang of nostalgia. Having someone who loves you, IвҖҷve decided, is a good reason to have kids.

Olga Khazan is a staff writer at The Atlantic. The is essay was adapted from her forthcoming book, Me, but Better: The Science and Promise of Personality Change.

entry.title: Why don't Companies Want you to Repair your Stuff?
entry.content:
Why donвҖҷt Companies Want you to Repair your Stuff?

For centuries, to build any device, artisans had to painstakingly cut every screw , bolt , or nut by hand.

But all this changed in the 1790s, when British metalworker Henry Maudslay developed this highly precise lathe .

Suddenly, these previously handmade components could be reproduced mechanically and at scale .

This may seem like a simple innovation, but it had a profound effect on the world.

The standardization of these parts helped usher in the Industrial Revolution.

From cars to home appliances, the 20th century was defined by mass production and readily available interchangeable parts.

This made repairing items relatively easy.

If your shower head gasket failed or the hose for your washing machine sprang a leak , a hardware store likely carried replacements.

Today, interchangeable parts are as important as ever for mass production.

However, some companies are working hard to prevent consumers from swapping out components on their own.

In many cases, repair can only be done by the original manufacturer, if at all .

With limited repair options available, we end up buying new and throwing more items out.

This is especially true for electronics.

In 2022, we discarded about 62 million tons of e-waste, along with billions of dollarsвҖҷ worth of precious metals inside them.

So how exactly do companies prevent repair?

Some make it physically more difficult to fix items.

They can glue parts in place rather than using screws.

Others limit the information they share with consumers, like blocking public access to product information and schematics .

During the pandemic, for instance, when hospitals struggled to maintain breaking medical equipment, the repair company iFixit compiled a comprehensive database of repair manuals for hospitals to use.

Yet manufacturers like Steris fought for them to be taken down .

Yet perhaps the most important, yet least obvious, way companies limit repair is by preventing the interchangeability of parts.

In a process known as parts pairing, companies assign individual parts like screens, batteries, or sensors a unique serial number.

The deviceвҖҷs internal software can then detect if those components are replaced and limit their functionality as a result.

If you swap the screen on two brand new iPhones, for example, the automatic brightness adjustment will no longer work.

In other cases, unauthorized replacements can cause the device to stop functioning altogether.

If a farmer uses an independent shop to repair certain parts in a John Deere tractor, it wonвҖҷt operate until a company technician authenticates the new part using authorized software.

With only a limited number of company technicians available, this can easily create a bottleneck , costing farmers precious time and money, and even jeopardizing crops.

Manufacturers can also use software to give devices predetermined end-of-life dates , after which they no longer receive important updates and slowly become unusable.

All this gives manufacturers unprecedented control over the products we buy and limits the way we repair them.

Critics liken these tactics to a form of planned obsolescence , a manufacturing strategy where a product is deliberately designed to fail to keep both demand and profits high.

But companies pose two main arguments in defense of these practices.

First, they claim that giving consumers and third-party repair shops access to their software could create cybersecurity risks.

They also argue that they could be liable if a product malfunctions after a third party or consumer repair.

A shoddy repair to a medical device, for instance, could have deadly consequences, and the manufacturer could be blamed.

However, major reports, like those from the U.S. Federal Trade Commission and the Food and Drug Administration, find little support for these company claims.

Parts pairing and repair-limiting practices are now being closely scrutinized in courts all over the world, as consumers fight for the right to repair.

States, including Colorado and Oregon, have passed laws banning parts pairing.

Community-led movements have also taken root.

Over 2,500 cities across the globe, from Amsterdam to Boise to Bangalore, have established repair cafes .

Here, people trade and share their knowledge, reinvigorating a culture of repair at a time when it often feels easier to toss our things than fix them.

Vocabulary, Phrases and Sentences

WordChinese DefinitionPhonetic Symbol
artisanе·ҘеҢ пјҢжҠҖе·Ҙ[ЛҲЙ‘ЛҗtЙӘzn]

entry.title: Lesson 6 Table Manners in Anglo-America
entry.content:
Table Manners in Anglo-America

"Oh, no! Here I am at an American familyвҖҷs home at the dinner table. There are all kinds of plates, saucers, cups, and silverware at my place. Which should I use for which food? Should I sit down first or wait for the host to invite me? Should I have brought a gift? Someone please tell me what to do! "

Have you ever been in or had a nightmare about this situation? DonвҖҷt worry! This article will help steer you through the rocks and reefs of Anglo-American table manners so that if you are ever abroad in Canada or the United States, or at someoneвҖҷs home from one of those countries, you will feel right at home.

It is important to distinguish what kind of occasion you will be attending before you plan for a pleasant evening. Most Anglo-Americans enjoy entertaining at home, but they donвҖҷt enjoy stuffy , formal dinners. They invite their friends over for a fun evening, not as a test of oneвҖҷs knowledge of cultural traditions. If, however, you are invited to a formal affair, such as a so-called вҖңsit-downвҖқ dinner, you may want to know in advance some basic rules of вҖңblack tieвҖқ etiquette .

The first thing to remember when attending a dinner at a Western home is that you are the guest and that you are a foreigner. No one will invite you if he does not really want you to enter his "castle;вҖҷ so you can be sure that you are wanted. Additionally, as you do not come from the same country or culture as your host, he or she or they will surely be aware of this, and will be very forgiving if you unintentionally do or say something which would otherwise offend them .Keeping these two simple tips in mind should greatly ease your concern about being present at a dinner in someone elseвҖҷs home.

Before arriving at your hostвҖҷs home, you may want to make sure of three things. First, be a few minutes late, say, about five to ten minutes if possible. Never be early, as the host may not have everything prepared yet. Nor should you be more than 20 minutes late. Your host may begin to worry about whether you are able to attend the dinner or not. Next, as to whether to bring a gift, in most informal gatherings, it is not necessary. If you like, you can bring some fruit or sweets, or, especially if there is a hostess, some flowers. These are thoughtful cheerful gifts sure to please. Do not bring alcoholic beverages unless you are sure of your hostвҖҷs or hostessвҖҷs preferences in drinks. Above all, do not spend a lot of money, and never give money. As we say in English, " itвҖҷs the thought that counts !вҖҷ finally, wear comfortable clothing. One can overdress as well as appear sloppy . For a special occasion or religious holiday, such as a retirement party or Christmas, a tie and jacket would be suitable for the gentlemen and a dress or sweater and skirt for the ladies.

For more formal affairs, you will probably be told what to wear, such as "formal dress requested,вҖқ etc. A tie and jacket or tuxedo for the gents and an evening gown for the ladies would be in order here. If you are unsure what to wear, you can always ask the host. Gifts are seldom appropriate for these affairs, unless for a wedding reception, at which gifts are more customary than cash.

Your host in his home will usually motion you where to sit. At formal gatherings, name cards are sometimes provided, or you will be told where to sit. Do not be alarmed by a great deal of cutlery : simply start from the outside and work your way in. Formal affairs often have several courses of food with the appropriate cutlery for each dish. There is no harm in checking with your neighbor to see what implement he is using. After all, " When in Rome, do as the Romans do :вҖҷ It is customary to ask others to pass dishes to you for self-serving; at a formal dinner party, there is usually catering (service). Again, do not hesitate to ask others for information or advice. They are usually pleased to help you.

The most important piece of advice is this: enjoy yourself. No host enjoys seeing nervous or fearful guests who are struggling to вҖңdo the right thingвҖқ at his home or expensive formal dinner party. Watch others or ask for their advice, and join in the conversation and good times as best you can .If you do, after the first such evening out, you will certainly look forward to the next!

вҖңе“ҰпјҢдёҚпјҒжҲ‘зҺ°еңЁиә«еӨ„дёҖдёӘзҫҺеӣҪе®¶еәӯзҡ„йӨҗжЎҢж—ҒгҖӮжҲ‘йқўеүҚж‘Ҷж”ҫзқҖеҗ„з§Қеҗ„ж ·зҡ„зӣҳеӯҗгҖҒиҢ¶зўҹгҖҒжқҜеӯҗе’Ңй“¶йӨҗе…·гҖӮеҗғдёҚеҗҢзҡ„йЈҹзү©иҜҘз”Ёе“Әд»¶йӨҗе…·е‘ўпјҹжҲ‘еә”иҜҘе…ҲеқҗдёӢиҝҳжҳҜзӯүдё»дәәйӮҖиҜ·е‘ўпјҹжҲ‘иҜҘеёҰдёӘзӨјзү©жқҘеҗ—пјҹи°ҒиғҪе‘ҠиҜүжҲ‘иҜҘжҖҺд№ҲеҒҡе•ҠпјҒвҖқ

дҪ жҳҜеҗҰжӣҫз»ҸеҺҶиҝҮиҝҷз§Қжғ…еҶөжҲ–иҖ…еҒҡиҝҮе…ідәҺе®ғзҡ„еҷ©жўҰе‘ўпјҹеҲ«жӢ…еҝғпјҒжң¬ж–Үе°Ҷеё®еҠ©дҪ йҒҝејҖиӢұзҫҺйӨҗжЎҢзӨјд»Әзҡ„жҡ—зӨҒйҷ©ж»©пјҢиҝҷж ·дёҖжқҘпјҢеҰӮжһңдҪ иә«еӨ„еҠ жӢҝеӨ§жҲ–зҫҺеӣҪпјҢжҲ–иҖ…еңЁжқҘиҮӘиҝҷдёӨдёӘеӣҪе®¶д№ӢдёҖзҡ„жҹҗдёӘдәәе®¶йҮҢпјҢдҪ дјҡж„ҹи§үе®ҫиҮіеҰӮеҪ’гҖӮ

еңЁдҪ дёәдёҖдёӘж„үеҝ«зҡ„еӨңжҷҡеҒҡеҮҶеӨҮд№ӢеүҚпјҢеҢәеҲҶдҪ иҰҒеҸӮеҠ зҡ„жҳҜе“Әз§ҚеңәеҗҲеҫҲйҮҚиҰҒгҖӮеӨ§еӨҡж•°иӢұзҫҺдәәеЈ«е–ңж¬ўеңЁе®¶жӢӣеҫ…е®ўдәәпјҢдҪҶд»–д»¬дёҚе–ңж¬ўеҸӨжқҝгҖҒжӯЈејҸзҡ„жҷҡе®ҙгҖӮд»–д»¬йӮҖиҜ·жңӢеҸӢжқҘе…ұеәҰж¬ўд№җж—¶е…үпјҢиҖҢдёҚжҳҜжқҘиҖғйӘҢжҹҗдәәеҜ№ж–ҮеҢ–дј з»ҹзҡ„дәҶи§ЈгҖӮз„¶иҖҢпјҢеҰӮжһңдҪ иў«йӮҖиҜ·еҸӮеҠ дёҖдёӘжӯЈејҸжҙ»еҠЁпјҢжҜ”еҰӮжүҖи°“зҡ„вҖңеқҗдёӢжқҘеҗғвҖқзҡ„жҷҡе®ҙпјҢдҪ еҸҜиғҪжғіжҸҗеүҚдәҶи§ЈдёҖдәӣвҖңй»‘йўҶз»“вҖқзӨјд»Әзҡ„еҹәжң¬и§„еҲҷгҖӮ

еҪ“дҪ еңЁиҘҝж–№дәәе®¶дёӯеҸӮеҠ жҷҡе®ҙж—¶пјҢйҰ–е…ҲиҰҒи®°дҪҸзҡ„жҳҜдҪ жҳҜе®ўдәәпјҢиҖҢдё”дҪ жҳҜдёӘеӨ–еӣҪдәәгҖӮеҰӮжһңдё»дәәдёҚжҳҜзңҹеҝғжғійӮҖиҜ·дҪ иҝӣе…Ҙд»–зҡ„вҖңеҹҺе ЎвҖқпјҢе°ұдёҚдјҡйӮҖиҜ·дҪ пјҢжүҖд»ҘдҪ еҸҜд»ҘзЎ®е®ҡдҪ жҳҜеҸ—ж¬ўиҝҺзҡ„гҖӮжӯӨеӨ–пјҢз”ұдәҺдҪ е’Ңдё»дәәжқҘиҮӘдёҚеҗҢзҡ„еӣҪе®¶жҲ–ж–ҮеҢ–пјҢд»–жҲ–еҘ№жҲ–д»–д»¬иӮҜе®ҡдјҡж„ҸиҜҶеҲ°иҝҷдёҖзӮ№пјҢеҰӮжһңдҪ ж— ж„ҸдёӯеҒҡдәҶжҲ–иҜҙдәҶжҹҗдәӣеҗҰеҲҷдјҡеҶ’зҠҜд»–д»¬зҡ„дәӢжғ…пјҢд»–д»¬дјҡйқһеёёе®Ҫе®№гҖӮи®°дҪҸиҝҷдёӨдёӘз®ҖеҚ•зҡ„иҰҒзӮ№еә”иҜҘдјҡеӨ§еӨ§еҮҸиҪ»дҪ еҜ№еңЁеҲ«дәәе®¶еҸӮеҠ жҷҡе®ҙзҡ„жӢ…еҝ§гҖӮ

еңЁеҲ°иҫҫдё»дәәе®¶д№ӢеүҚпјҢдҪ еҸҜиғҪжғізЎ®и®Өдёүд»¶дәӢгҖӮйҰ–е…ҲпјҢзЁҚеҫ®иҝҹеҲ°еҮ еҲҶй’ҹпјҢжҜ”еҰӮиҜҙпјҢеҰӮжһңеҸҜиғҪзҡ„иҜқпјҢеӨ§зәҰиҝҹеҲ°дә”еҲ°еҚҒеҲҶй’ҹгҖӮз»қдёҚиҰҒж—©еҲ°пјҢеӣ дёәдё»дәәеҸҜиғҪиҝҳжІЎжңүжҠҠдёҖеҲҮйғҪеҮҶеӨҮеҘҪгҖӮдҪ д№ҹдёҚеә”иҜҘиҝҹеҲ°и¶…иҝҮдәҢеҚҒеҲҶй’ҹгҖӮдҪ зҡ„дё»дәәеҸҜиғҪдјҡејҖе§ӢжӢ…еҝғдҪ жҳҜеҗҰиғҪжқҘеҸӮеҠ жҷҡе®ҙгҖӮе…¶ж¬ЎпјҢе…ідәҺжҳҜеҗҰеёҰзӨјзү©пјҢеңЁеӨ§еӨҡж•°йқһжӯЈејҸиҒҡдјҡдёҠпјҢиҝҷдёҚжҳҜеҝ…йңҖзҡ„гҖӮеҰӮжһңдҪ ж„ҝж„ҸпјҢдҪ еҸҜд»ҘеёҰдәӣж°ҙжһңжҲ–зі–жһңпјҢжҲ–иҖ…пјҢзү№еҲ«жҳҜеҰӮжһңжңүеҘідё»дәәзҡ„иҜқпјҢеёҰдәӣиҠұгҖӮиҝҷдәӣйғҪжҳҜиҙҙеҝғеҸҲи®Ёе–ңзҡ„зӨјзү©гҖӮйҷӨйқһдҪ зЎ®е®ҡдё»дәәжҲ–еҘідё»дәәеҜ№йҘ®е“Ғзҡ„е–ңеҘҪпјҢеҗҰеҲҷдёҚиҰҒеёҰй…’зІҫйҘ®ж–ҷгҖӮжңҖйҮҚиҰҒзҡ„жҳҜпјҢдёҚиҰҒиҠұеҫҲеӨҡй’ұпјҢиҖҢдё”з»қеҜ№дёҚиҰҒз»ҷй’ұгҖӮе°ұеғҸжҲ‘д»¬еңЁиӢұиҜӯйҮҢиҜҙзҡ„пјҢвҖңеҝғж„ҸжңҖйҮҚиҰҒпјҒвҖқжңҖеҗҺпјҢз©ҝиҲ’йҖӮзҡ„иЎЈжңҚгҖӮдёҖдёӘдәәж—ўеҸҜиғҪз©ҝеҫ—иҝҮдәҺи®Із©¶пјҢд№ҹеҸҜиғҪжҳҫеҫ—йӮӢйҒўгҖӮеҜ№дәҺзү№ж®ҠеңәеҗҲжҲ–е®—ж•ҷиҠӮж—ҘпјҢжҜ”еҰӮйҖҖдј‘жҙҫеҜ№жҲ–еңЈиҜһиҠӮпјҢз”·еЈ«з©ҝйўҶеёҰе’ҢеӨ№е…ӢдјҡеҫҲеҗҲйҖӮпјҢеҘіеЈ«з©ҝиҝһиЎЈиЈҷжҲ–жҜӣиЎЈеҠ иЈҷеӯҗгҖӮ

еҜ№дәҺжӣҙжӯЈејҸзҡ„жҙ»еҠЁпјҢдҪ еҸҜиғҪдјҡиў«е‘ҠзҹҘиҜҘз©ҝд»Җд№ҲпјҢжҜ”еҰӮвҖңиҰҒжұӮзқҖжӯЈиЈ…вҖқзӯүзӯүгҖӮиҝҷйҮҢз”·еЈ«з©ҝйўҶеёҰе’ҢеӨ№е…ӢжҲ–ж— е°ҫзӨјжңҚпјҢеҘіеЈ«з©ҝжҷҡзӨјжңҚгҖӮеҰӮжһңдҪ дёҚзЎ®е®ҡз©ҝд»Җд№ҲпјҢдҪ жҖ»жҳҜеҸҜд»Ҙй—®дё»дәәгҖӮеңЁиҝҷдәӣжҙ»еҠЁдёӯпјҢзӨјзү©еҫҲе°‘еҗҲйҖӮпјҢйҷӨйқһжҳҜе©ҡзӨјжӢӣеҫ…дјҡпјҢеңЁе©ҡзӨјдёҠйҖҒзӨјзү©жҜ”йҖҒзҺ°йҮ‘жӣҙеёёи§ҒгҖӮ

еңЁд»–е®¶пјҢдё»дәәйҖҡеёёдјҡзӨәж„ҸдҪ еқҗеңЁе“ӘйҮҢгҖӮеңЁжӯЈејҸиҒҡдјҡдёҠпјҢжңүж—¶дјҡжҸҗдҫӣеҗҚзүҮпјҢжҲ–иҖ…дјҡе‘ҠиҜүдҪ еқҗеңЁе“ӘйҮҢгҖӮдёҚиҰҒиў«еӨ§йҮҸзҡ„йӨҗе…·еҗ“еҲ°пјҡеҸӘйңҖд»ҺеӨ–йқўејҖе§ӢпјҢжҢүйЎәеәҸеҫҖйҮҢз”ЁгҖӮжӯЈејҸжҙ»еҠЁйҖҡеёёжңүеҘҪеҮ йҒ“иҸңпјҢжҜҸйҒ“иҸңйғҪй…ҚжңүеҗҲйҖӮзҡ„йӨҗе…·гҖӮеҗ‘дҪ зҡ„йӮ»еә§иҜўй—®д»–жӯЈеңЁдҪҝз”Ёд»Җд№ҲйӨҗе…·е№¶ж— еқҸеӨ„гҖӮжҜ•з«ҹпјҢвҖңе…Ҙд№ЎйҡҸдҝ—вҖқгҖӮжҢүд№ дҝ—пјҢдәәд»¬дјҡиҜ·еҲ«дәәжҠҠиҸңйҖ’з»ҷиҮӘе·ұд»ҘдҫҝиҮӘеҸ–йЈҹзү©пјӣеңЁжӯЈејҸжҷҡе®ҙиҒҡдјҡдёҠпјҢйҖҡеёёдјҡжңүйӨҗйҘ®жңҚеҠЎпјҲдҫҚиҖ…жңҚеҠЎпјүгҖӮеҗҢж ·пјҢдёҚиҰҒзҠ№иұ«еҗ‘еҲ«дәәеҜ»жұӮдҝЎжҒҜжҲ–е»әи®®гҖӮд»–д»¬йҖҡеёёеҫҲд№җж„Ҹеё®еҠ©дҪ гҖӮ

жңҖйҮҚиҰҒзҡ„дёҖжқЎе»әи®®жҳҜпјҡе°Ҫжғ…дә«еҸ—гҖӮжІЎжңүе“ӘдёӘдё»дәәе–ңж¬ўзңӢеҲ°зҙ§еј жҲ–е®іжҖ•зҡ„е®ўдәәеңЁд»–е®¶йҮҢжҲ–жҳӮиҙөзҡ„жӯЈејҸжҷҡе®ҙиҒҡдјҡдёҠеҠӘеҠӣвҖңеҒҡеҜ№дәӢжғ…вҖқгҖӮи§ӮеҜҹеҲ«дәәжҲ–иҖ…еҗ‘д»–д»¬иҜ·ж•ҷпјҢе°ҪдҪ жүҖиғҪеҠ е…Ҙи°ҲиҜқе№¶дә«еҸ—зҫҺеҘҪж—¶е…үгҖӮеҰӮжһңдҪ иҝҷж ·еҒҡдәҶпјҢеңЁз¬¬дёҖж¬Ўиҝҷж ·зҡ„еӨ–еҮәжҷҡе®ҙд№ӢеҗҺпјҢдҪ иӮҜе®ҡдјҡжңҹеҫ…дёӢдёҖж¬ЎпјҒ

Vocabulary & Idioms

  • suacerвҖ”вҖ”е°ҸиҢ¶зўҹ
  • steerвҖ”вҖ”еј•еҜј
  • the rocks and reefsвҖ”вҖ”йҮҚйҮҚйҡңзўҚ
    • rockвҖ”вҖ”еІ©зҹі
    • reefвҖ”вҖ”жҡ—зӨҒ
  • distinguishвҖ”вҖ”иҫЁеҲ«
  • stuffyвҖ”вҖ”жІүй—·зҡ„
  • in advanceвҖ”вҖ”жҸҗеүҚ
  • etiquetteвҖ”вҖ”зӨјд»Ә
  • some basic rules of вҖңblack tieвҖқ etiquetteвҖ”вҖ”й»‘йўҶеёҰзӨјд»Әзҡ„дёҖдәӣеҹәжң¬и§„еҲҷ
  • be aware ofвҖ”вҖ”ж„ҸиҜҶеҲ°
  • thoughtfulвҖ”вҖ”дҪ“иҙҙзҡ„пјҢе‘ЁеҲ°зҡ„
  • ItвҖҷs the thought that countsвҖ”вҖ”еҝғж„ҸжңҖйҮҚиҰҒ
  • overdressвҖ”вҖ”иҝҮеәҰжү“жү®
  • appropriateвҖ”вҖ”еҗҲйҖӮзҡ„
  • customaryвҖ”вҖ”жғҜдҫӢзҡ„
  • motionвҖ”вҖ”д»Ҙе§ҝжҖҒжҲ–жүӢеҠҝзӨәж„Ҹ
  • culteryвҖ”вҖ”еҲҖеҸүзұ»йӨҗе…·
  • There is no harm in + V-ingвҖ”вҖ”еҒҡвҖҰвҖҰж— еҰЁ
  • implementвҖ”вҖ”еҷЁе…·
  • hesitateвҖ”вҖ”зҠ№иұ«
  • look forward toвҖ”вҖ”зӣјжңӣ

entry.title: дёҖдәӣиғҢжҷҜзҹҘиҜҶ
entry.content:
ж— жңәеӣәжҖҒз”өи§ЈиҙЁеҹәзЎҖжҖ§иҙЁ

еӨҡе°әеәҰзҰ»еӯҗдј иҫ“

еҺҹеӯҗе°әеәҰ

  • зҰ»еӯҗжү©ж•Ји·Ҝеҫ„дёҺзҺҜеўғпјҡ

йҳізҰ»еӯҗжІҝзқҖжңүеҲ©зҡ„и·Ҝеҫ„жү©ж•ЈпјҢиҝҷдәӣи·Ҝеҫ„з”ұйҳҙзҰ»еӯҗжһ„жҲҗзҡ„жЎҶжһ¶жүҖеҶіе®ҡгҖӮйҳізҰ»еӯҗжү©ж•ЈеҸҜзңӢдҪңжҳҜеңЁеҹәжҖҒзЁіе®ҡдҪҚзӮ№дёҺдёӯй—ҙдәҡзЁідҪҚзӮ№д№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„зҰ»еӯҗи·іи·ғпјҢиғҪйҮҸдё»иҰҒз”ұе…¶еұҖйғЁзҰ»еӯҗй…ҚдҪҚзҺҜеўғеҶіе®ҡпјҲжҷ¶дҪ“дёӯйҖҡеёёдёәеӣӣй…ҚдҪҚе’Ңе…ӯй…ҚдҪҚпјүпјҢдҪ“еҝғз«Ӣж–№йҳҙзҰ»еӯҗжЎҶжһ¶е°ұжҳҜдёҖз§Қеёёи§Ғзҡ„дјҳз§ҖзҰ»еӯҗеҜјдҪ“жЎҶжһ¶пјҢеӣ дёәиҝҷз§ҚжЎҶжһ¶иғҪи®©зҰ»еӯҗеңЁзӣёйӮ»еӣӣйқўдҪ“дҪҚзӮ№й—ҙзӣҙжҺҘи·іи·ғпјҢдё”жҙ»еҢ–иғҪEaE_aEaвҖӢиҫғдҪҺпјҢж— йңҖз»ҸиҝҮй«ҳ EaE_aEaвҖӢ зҡ„еӣӣйқўдҪ“ - е…«йқўдҪ“и·іи·ғиҝҮзЁӢ(fcc, hcp)

  • йҳізҰ»еӯҗиҝҒз§»жңәеҲ¶

    • з©әдҪҚжү©ж•Ј
    • зӣҙжҺҘй—ҙйҡҷ
    • еҚҸеҗҢжү©ж•Ј

  • зҰ»еӯҗз”өеҜјзҺҮзӣёе…іеӣ зҙ 

Пғ=qnu=Пғ0TmeвҲ’EakBT\sigma=qnu=\sigma_0T^me^{-\frac{E_a}{k_BT}}Пғ=qnu=Пғ0вҖӢTmeвҲ’kBвҖӢTEaвҖӢвҖӢ

  • зјәйҷ·дёҺзҰ»еӯҗдј еҜјзҡ„е…ізі»

еңЁжң¬еҫҒзҠ¶жҖҒдёӢпјҢз§»еҠЁеёҰз”өзү©з§Қзҡ„жө“еәҰ n3n_3n3вҖӢ дёҺжё©еәҰжңүе…іпјҢе№¶дё”е’Ңз©әдҪҚеҸҠй—ҙйҡҷзҡ„зјәйҷ·еҪўжҲҗиғҪ EfE_fEfвҖӢ зӣёе…ігҖӮзҰ»еӯҗжҺәжқӮеҸҜд»ҘеҲӣйҖ еҮәз”өиҚ·иЎҘеҒҝз©әдҪҚжҲ–иҖ…й—ҙйҡҷпјҢд»ҺиҖҢж”№еҸҳзјәйҷ·зҡ„жө“еәҰпјҢдҪҶд№ҹеҸҜиғҪеҪұе“ҚзҰ»еӯҗдј еҜјжңәеҲ¶пјҲеҰӮй”Ӯи¶…зҰ»еӯҗеҜјдҪ“пјҲLISICONпјүеһӢзҡ„еӨ§йҮҸзҰ»еӯҗжҺәжқӮеҸҜиғҪдҪҝдј еҜјжңәеҲ¶еҸҳдёәвҖңи¶…зҰ»еӯҗвҖқжңәеҲ¶пјүгҖӮ

  • е…¶д»–еҪұе“ҚзҰ»еӯҗиҝҒз§»зҡ„еӣ зҙ 
    • жңәжў°жҖ§иғҪиҫғиҪҜзҡ„йҳҙзҰ»еӯҗжЎҶжһ¶
      • дј еҲ°жҙ»еҢ–иғҪжӣҙдҪҺпјҢжңүеҠ©дәҺзҰ»еӯҗжү©ж•Ј
      • иҫғдҪҺзҡ„жҷ¶ж јжҢҜеҠЁйў‘зҺҮдјҡеҜјиҮҙиҫғдҪҺзҡ„и·іи·ғе°қиҜ•йў‘зҺҮОҪo\nu_oОҪoвҖӢе’ҢиҝҒз§»зҶөО”Sm\Delta S_mО”SmвҖӢеҮҸе°ҸпјҢд»ҺиҖҢйҷҚдҪҺйҳҝж°Ҹж–№зЁӢзҡ„жҢҮеүҚеӣ еӯҗ

еӣәжҖҒеҗҲжҲҗжі•иҪҜеҢ–еӯҰжі•жңәжў°еҢ–еӯҰжі•
е·Ҙиүәе°ҶеҺҹж–ҷзІүжң«ж··еҗҲеҗҺй«ҳжё©зғ§з»“пјҲйҖҡеёё > 600В°CпјүеңЁж¶ІжҖҒжә¶еүӮпјҲеҰӮд№ҷи…ҲгҖҒж°ҙпјүдёӯжә¶и§ЈеҺҹж–ҷпјҢйҖҡиҝҮжІүж·ҖгҖҒжә¶иғ¶ - еҮқиғ¶зӯүеҸҚеә”з”ҹжҲҗеүҚй©ұдҪ“пјҢеҶҚз»Ҹе№ІзҮҘе’ҢйҖҖзҒ«йҖҡиҝҮзҗғзЈЁпјҲй«ҳиғҪзў°ж’һпјүеј•еҸ‘еӣәжҖҒеҸҚеә”
дјҳзӮ№з®ҖеҚ•жҳ“иЎҢпјҢеҸҜж·¬зҒ«дҝқз•ҷй«ҳжё©зӣёдҪҺжё©еҗҲжҲҗпјҲ<300В°CпјүпјҢзІҫзЎ®жҺ§еҲ¶зәізұіз»“жһ„дҪҺжё©еҗҲжҲҗпјҲе®Өжё©иҮі 400В°CпјүпјҢжҷ¶зІ’з»ҶеҢ–иҮізәізұізә§
зјәзӮ№й«ҳжё©еҜјиҮҙжҳ“жҢҘеҸ‘жҲҗеҲҶпјҢеҸҜиғҪеј•е…ҘжқӮиҙЁжә¶еүӮеӣһж”¶жҲҗжң¬й«ҳпјҢеүҚй©ұдҪ“зәҜеәҰдҫқиө–жә¶еүӮзәҜеәҰе·ҘиүәеҸӮж•°пјҲзҗғзЈЁж—¶й—ҙгҖҒиҪ¬йҖҹпјүеҜ№дә§зү©еҪұе“ҚеӨ§пјҢйҮҚеӨҚжҖ§е·®пјҢеҸҜиғҪеј•е…ҘжқӮиҙЁ
жЎҲдҫӢзҹіжҰҙзҹіеһӢ LLZOпјҲLi7La3Zr2O12\rm{Li_7La_3Zr_2O_{12}}Li7вҖӢLa3вҖӢZr2вҖӢO12вҖӢпјүзҡ„еҗҲжҲҗж°ҙзғӯжі•еҗҲжҲҗNa3SbS4\rm{Na_3SbS_4}Na3вҖӢSbS4вҖӢзҗғзЈЁжі•еҲ¶еӨҮLi11Si2PS12\rm{Li_{11}Si_2PS_{12}}Li11вҖӢSi2вҖӢPS12вҖӢ

иҮҙеҜҶеҢ–жҠҖжңҜпјҡе°ҶеҗҲжҲҗеҗҺзҡ„зІүжң«еҠ е·ҘжҲҗй«ҳеҜҶеәҰиҶңжҲ–зүҮпјҢд»ҘйҷҚдҪҺз”өйҳ»е№¶жҸҗй«ҳжңәжў°ејәеәҰ

зғ§з»“зҒ«иҠұзӯүзҰ»еӯҗзғ§з»“еҶ·еҺӢ
е·ҘиүәеёёеҺӢжҲ–еҠ еҺӢдёӢпјҲеҰӮзғӯеҺӢпјүе°ҶзІүжң«еңЁй«ҳжё©пјҲйҖҡеёё > 500В°CпјүдёӢзғ§з»“и„үеҶІз”өжөҒеҠ зғӯзІүжң«иҮізӣ®ж Үжё©еәҰпјҲеҰӮ LLZO еңЁ 1000В°CпјүпјҢеҗҢж—¶ж–ҪеҠ еҺӢеҠӣпјҲ50вҖ“100 MPaпјүе®Өжё©дёӢеҜ№зІүжң«ж–ҪеҠ й«ҳеҺӢпјҲеҰӮ 500 MPaпјүеҪўжҲҗзҙ еқҜпјҢеҶҚзғ§з»“
зјәзӮ№й«ҳжё©еҸҜиғҪеҜјиҮҙжҷ¶з•ҢжһҗеҮәжқӮиҙЁжҲ–иҖ…дёҚзЁіе®ҡи®ҫеӨҮжҲҗжң¬й«ҳпјҢи§„жЁЎеҢ–з”ҹдә§еҸ—йҷҗ

йӣҶжҲҗе·Ҙиүә

и–„иҶңжі•еҶ·еҺӢйӣҶжҲҗж№ҝжі•зәёжөҶзҶ”дҪ“й“ёйҖ 
е·Ҙиүәжә…е°„гҖҒи’ёй•ҖжҲ–и„үеҶІжҝҖе…үжІүз§ҜпјҲPLDпјүеҲ¶еӨҮзәізұізә§з”өи§ЈиҙЁиҶңе°Ҷз”өи§ЈиҙЁзІүжң«дёҺз”өжһҒжқҗж–ҷпјҲеҰӮ LiCoOвӮӮпјүж··еҗҲеҗҺеҶ·еҺӢпјҲ1вҖ“5 GPaпјүжҲҗеӨҡеұӮз»“жһ„е°Ҷз”өи§ЈиҙЁзІүжң«еҲҶж•ЈеңЁжә¶еүӮпјҲеҰӮ NMPпјүдёӯпјҢдёҺз”өжһҒжқҗж–ҷгҖҒзІҳз»“еүӮж··еҗҲжҲҗжөҶж–ҷпјҢж¶ӮиҰҶеҗҺе№ІзҮҘе°ҶдҪҺзҶ”зӮ№з”өи§ЈиҙЁпјҲеҰӮ LiвӮӮOHClпјҢзҶ”зӮ№вүҲ400В°CпјүеҠ зғӯиҮіж¶ІжҖҒеҗҺеҖ’е…ҘжЁЎе…·пјҢеҶ·еҚҙеӣәеҢ–
дјҳзӮ№еҺҡеәҰеҸҜжҺ§пјҢйҖӮеҗҲеҫ®еһӢз”өжұ пјҢй«ҳиҮҙеҜҶжҖ§пјҢд»ӢйқўжҺҘи§ҰиүҜеҘҪйҖӮеҗҲи§„жЁЎеҢ–з”ҹдә§пјҢжә¶еүӮиҫ…еҠ©еҪўжҲҗеқҮеҢҖз•ҢйқўпјҢжҸҗй«ҳзҰ»еӯҗжҺҘи§Ұйқўз§ҜдёҖжӯҘжҲҗеһӢпјҢйҖӮеҗҲеӨҚжқӮеҪўзҠ¶
зјәзӮ№и®ҫеӨҮжҳӮиҙөпјҢйҡҫд»Ҙи§„жЁЎеҢ–з”ҹдә§з•ҢйқўжҺҘи§Ұйқўз§ҜдёҚи¶іпјҢеҜјиҮҙй«ҳйҳ»жҠ—зЎ«еҢ–зү©еҜ№ж°ҙж•Ҹж„ҹпјҢйңҖдёҘж јжҺ§еҲ¶зҺҜеўғж№ҝеәҰд»…йҖӮз”ЁдәҺдҪҺзҶ”зӮ№жқҗж–ҷпјҢеә”з”ЁиҢғеӣҙжңүйҷҗ

е·ҘиүәеҜ№зҰ»еӯҗз”өеҜјзҺҮзҡ„еҪұе“ҚеҜ№жңәжў°жҖ§иғҪзҡ„еҪұе“ҚеҜ№з•Ңйқўзҡ„еҪұе“Қ
еӣәжҖҒеҗҲжҲҗй«ҳжё©еҸҜиғҪдҝғиҝӣз»“жҷ¶пјҢжҸҗй«ҳз”өеҜјзҺҮпјҲеҰӮ LLZOпјүжҷ¶зІ’зІ—еӨ§пјҢж–ӯиЈӮйҹ§жҖ§иҫғдҪҺпјҲеҰӮ LLZO зҡ„ KвӮҒc=1.2 MPaгғ»mВ№/ВІпјүжҷ¶з•ҢжқӮиҙЁеҜјиҮҙй«ҳз”өйҳ»пјҲеҰӮ LLZO зҡ„жҷ¶з•Ңз”өйҳ»еҚ жҜ” > 50%пјү
жңәжў°еҢ–еӯҰжі•зәізұіжҷ¶зІ’еўһеҠ жҷ¶з•Ңдј еҜјпјҲеҰӮ LiвӮғPSвӮ„зҡ„ Пғ=2 mS/cmпјүжҷ¶зІ’з»ҶеҢ–жҸҗй«ҳж–ӯиЈӮйҹ§жҖ§пјҲеҰӮ LiвӮҶPSвӮ…Cl зҡ„ KвӮҒc=0.8вҶ’1.5 MPaгғ»mВ№/ВІпјүз•ҢйқўжҺҘи§Ұйқўз§ҜеӨ§пјҢдҪҶжҷ¶з•Ңзјәйҷ·еҸҜиғҪеўһеҠ йҳ»жҠ—
SPS иҮҙеҜҶеҢ–й«ҳиҮҙеҜҶеәҰеҮҸе°‘еӯ”йҡҷпјҢжҸҗй«ҳ ПғпјҲеҰӮ LLZO зҡ„ Пғ=0.8 mS/cmпјүй«ҳиҮҙеҜҶеәҰйҷҚдҪҺиЈӮзә№жү©еұ•йЈҺйҷ©пјҲеҰӮ LLZO зҡ„еӯ”йҡҷзҺҮ < 1%пјүз•Ңйқўз»“еҗҲзҙ§еҜҶпјҢдҪҶеҸҜиғҪеӣ еҝ«йҖҹеҶ·еҚҙеј•е…Ҙеә”еҠӣ
ж№ҝжі•еҲ¶жөҶжә¶еүӮиҫ…еҠ©еҪўжҲҗеқҮеҢҖжҺҘи§ҰпјҢйҷҚдҪҺз•Ңйқўз”өйҳ»пјҲеҰӮ LiвӮҶPSвӮ…Cl/NCM зҡ„з•Ңйқўз”өйҳ» < 50 О©гғ»cmВІпјүзІҳз»“еүӮпјҲеҰӮ PVDFпјүеўһеҠ жҹ”жҖ§пјҢдҪҶйҷҚдҪҺжңәжў°ејәеәҰжә¶еүӮж®Ӣз•ҷеҸҜиғҪеҜјиҮҙз•ҢйқўеүҜеҸҚеә”пјҲеҰӮзЎ«еҢ–зү©ж°ҙи§Јпјү

е…ій”®жҢ‘жҲҳдёҺи§ЈеҶіж–№жЎҲ

жҢ‘жҲҳи§ЈеҶіж–№жЎҲ
й”Ӯжһқжҷ¶з©ҝйҖҸз”өи§ЈиҙЁжҸҗй«ҳз”өи§ЈиҙЁж–ӯиЈӮйҹ§жҖ§пјҲеҰӮзәізұіжҷ¶ LLZO зҡ„ KвӮҒc=2.5 MPaгғ»mВ№/ВІпјүпјҢйҷҚдҪҺз”өеӯҗз”өеҜјзҺҮпјҲ<10вҒ»вҒ№ S/cmпјү
з•Ңйқўйҳ»жҠ—еўһй•ҝи®ҫи®ЎиҮӘй’қеҢ–з•ҢйқўпјҲеҰӮ LiPON/LiвӮӮO еҸҢеұӮж¶ӮеұӮпјүпјҢдјҳеҢ–жҺҘи§Ұйқўз§ҜпјҲеҰӮзәізұійў—зІ’еӨҚеҗҲз”өжһҒпјү
й«ҳиғҪйҮҸеҜҶеәҰдёҺй«ҳеҠҹзҺҮжқғиЎЎејҖеҸ‘еҸҢзҰ»еӯҗдј еҜји·Ҝеҫ„пјҲеҰӮ LiвҒә/ з©әдҪҚеҚҸеҗҢиҝҒз§»пјүпјҢйҮҮз”Ёи¶…и–„з”өи§ЈиҙЁпјҲ<10 Ојmпјү
зЎ«еҢ–зү©з”өи§ЈиҙЁзҡ„зҺҜеўғж•Ҹж„ҹжҖ§иЎЁйқўеҢ…иҰҶ Al2O3\rm{Al_2O_3}Al2вҖӢO3вҖӢжҲ– Li3BO3\rm{Li_3BO_3}Li3вҖӢBO3вҖӢпјҢејҖеҸ‘ж°ҙзЁіе®ҡз”өи§ЈиҙЁпјҲеҰӮ Li3OCl\rm{Li_3OCl}Li3вҖӢOClпјү

жҪңеңЁзӘҒз ҙзӮ№

  1. е®Өжё©е…ЁеӣәжҖҒй’ з”өжұ 
    • йҮҮз”Ё Na3PS4\rm{Na_3PS_4}Na3вҖӢPS4вҖӢеҹәз”өи§ЈиҙЁдёҺ Na3V2(PO4)3\rm{Na_3V_2(PO_4)_3}Na3вҖӢV2вҖӢ(PO4вҖӢ)3вҖӢйҳҙжһҒпјҢе®һзҺ°иғҪйҮҸеҜҶеәҰ > 200 Wh/kgгҖӮ
